The validation popover on the Submit button will show 'You need to upload a List C document' to guide you."}, {"id": "cf6", "text": "What happens after you submit your W-4 form data in the electronic form renderer?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "The form data is emailed to the IRS directly"}, {"id": "b", "text": "The system fills the official government PDF with your data and sends it to DocuSeal for your legally binding e-signature", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "A manager must manually approve the submission before it takes effect"}, {"id": "d", "text": "The data is saved but you must print and sign a paper copy separately"}], "explanation": "For forms with DocuSeal integration and AcroField mapping configured, submitting triggers the fill-and-sign flow: your form data fills the official government PDF using AcroForm field mapping, then DocuSeal handles the legally binding e-signature via an embedded iframe. The signed PDF is stored securely in MinIO."}, {"id": "cf7", "text": "A form card on the Tax Forms page shows a red 'Required' badge. What does this mean?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "The form has a validation error that needs to be fixed"}, {"id": "b", "text": "The form has blocksJobAssignment enabled — you cannot be assigned to production jobs until it is completed", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "The form is past its annual renewal deadline"}, {"id": "d", "text": "An admin has flagged the form for immediate attention"}], "explanation": "The red 'Required' badge appears on forms where blocksJobAssignment is true on the template. This means the employee cannot be assigned to production jobs on the Kanban board until the form is completed. This is enforced server-side by the job assignment API."}, {"id": "cf8", "text": "You're filling out the state withholding form. How does Forge know which state form to show you?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "You manually select your state from a dropdown on the form"}, {"id": "b", "text": "It uses your home address from your employee profile"}, {"id": "c", "text": "It automatically determines the correct state form based on your assigned work location (CompanyLocation)", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "d", "text": "All employees fill out the same generic state form regardless of location"}], "explanation": "Forge determines the state withholding form based on your work location (WorkLocationId on ApplicationUser, which maps to a CompanyLocation). The system calls getMyStateDefinition which resolves the state code, checks if the state has income tax, and returns the appropriate state-specific form definition."}, {"id": "cf9", "text": "You want to save your progress on a compliance form and finish it later. What should you do?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Take a screenshot of the form and fill it out again later from scratch"}, {"id": "b", "text": "Click the Save Draft button — your form data is saved and you can return to continue where you left off", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "You cannot save progress — the form must be completed in one sitting"}, {"id": "d", "text": "Close the browser tab — the form auto-saves every keystroke"}], "explanation": "The Save Draft button (save icon) in the form renderer footer saves your current form data without submitting. When you return to the form later, your saved draft data is loaded automatically so you can continue where you left off."}, {"id": "cf10", "text": "What is the difference between 'Pending' and 'Opened' submission statuses?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Pending means the form has not been started; Opened means the employee is currently filling it out"}, {"id": "b", "text": "Pending means the submission was created but the DocuSeal signing link has not been opened yet; Opened means the signing link has been accessed", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "Pending means awaiting manager approval; Opened means the manager has reviewed it"}, {"id": "d", "text": "There is no difference — they are interchangeable statuses"}], "explanation": "Pending indicates a submission has been created (form data may be saved) but the DocuSeal signing ceremony has not been accessed. Opened indicates the employee has clicked the DocuSeal signing link and the signing iframe has been loaded. Both are intermediate states before Completed."}, {"id": "cf11", "text": "An employer (manager/admin) needs to complete Section 2 of an employee's I-9. What is the deadline?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Within 24 hours of the employee's hire date"}, {"id": "b", "text": "Within 3 business days of the employee's first day of work", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "Within 30 calendar days of the employee's start date"}, {"id": "d", "text": "There is no deadline — it can be completed at any time"}], "explanation": "Per federal regulations, I-9 Section 2 must be completed within 3 business days of the employee's first day of work. Forge tracks this as the i9Section2OverdueAt field, which is set when Section 1 is signed. The system enforces that Section 1 must be completed before Section 2 can begin."}, {"id": "cf12", "text": "You live in Texas and are asked to complete state withholding. What happens?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "You fill out the standard Texas withholding form"}, {"id": "b", "text": "The system shows that Texas has no state income tax and no form is required", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "You must fill out the federal W-4 again as a substitute"}, {"id": "d", "text": "An error is displayed because Texas is not a supported state"}], "explanation": "Texas is one of several states (along with Florida, Nevada, etc.) that have no state income tax. The state withholding system categorizes these as 'no_tax' states. The employee is informed that no state withholding form is required for their work location."}, {"id": "cf13", "text": "After completing a W-4, what information is shown in the submission summary card?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Your full Social Security Number, exact withholding dollar amounts, and employer EIN"}, {"id": "b", "text": "Filing Status, Multiple Jobs (Yes/No), and Exempt status — no sensitive data like SSN or dollar amounts", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "Nothing — the summary is blank for security reasons"}, {"id": "d", "text": "A link to download the raw form data JSON"}], "explanation": "The W-4 summary shows only non-sensitive fields: Filing Status, whether Multiple Jobs was selected (Yes/No), and Exempt status (Yes/No). Sensitive data like SSN and dollar amounts are not displayed back to the user. The W-4 is classified as a sensitive form (isSensitive = true), so even when resubmitting, previous data is not pre-filled."}, {"id": "cf14", "text": "What does 'form definition version pinning' mean for compliance form submissions?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "The form is locked to a specific CSS theme version"}, {"id": "b", "text": "Each submission records the exact form definition version it was filled against, so the form can be regenerated with the original layout even if the definition is later updated", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "The form can only be submitted once per calendar year"}, {"id": "d", "text": "The employer must approve the form version before it can be used"}], "explanation": "Each submission stores a formDefinitionVersionId that pins it to the exact form layout used when filling it out. This is important for compliance: if the IRS releases a new W-4 revision, previously submitted forms can still be viewed and regenerated using their original layout. The FormDefinitionVersion entity tracks template ID, state code, effective date, and the full form definition JSON."}, {"id": "cf15", "text": "How do you download a signed copy of a completed compliance form?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Go to Account > Tax Forms, click the completed form, and click the Download button (download icon)", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "b", "text": "Contact HR to email you a copy"}, {"id": "c", "text": "Navigate to the Files section and search for compliance documents"}, {"id": "d", "text": "Signed PDFs are not available for download — only viewable in the browser"}], "explanation": "On the completed form's detail page, click the Download button (download icon) to download the signed PDF. The system retrieves the file from MinIO storage and triggers a browser download with the form name as the filename (e.g., 'W-4 Employee\\'s Withholding Certificate.pdf'). The button shows a spinning sync icon while the download is in progress."}], "passingScore": 80, "shuffleOptions": true, "questionsPerQuiz": 8, "showExplanationsAfterSubmit": true}

title: Customer Returns Overview
summary: What customer returns (RMAs) are, the return lifecycle from Received to Closed, and how returns connect to sales orders, jobs, and inventory.
contentJson:
{"body": "## Customer Returns Overview

The Customer Returns module tracks returned goods from customers through a structured RMA (Return Merchandise Authorization) lifecycle. Every return is linked to the original job that produced the goods and optionally to a rework job created to correct the issue.

### What Is a Customer Return?

A customer return represents goods sent back by a customer due to defects, incorrect specifications, damage during shipping, or other quality issues. Each return is assigned a unique RMA number (e.g., RMA-00001) and tracked through a series of statuses until the issue is fully resolved and closed.

### The Return Lifecycle

Customer returns move through five statuses in a defined order:

1. **Received** — The return has been logged in the system. The customer's goods have arrived back at your facility. This is the initial status when a return is created.
2. **Under Inspection** — The returned goods are being inspected to determine the root cause of the issue. An inspector is assigned and inspection notes are recorded.
3. **Rework Ordered** — A rework job has been created to correct the defect. The rework job appears on the Kanban board and follows the normal production workflow. The return automatically moves to this status when a rework job is generated during creation.
4. **Resolved** — The issue has been addressed — either through rework, replacement, credit, or other corrective action. The return can be resolved via the resolve endpoint once the corrective action is complete.
5. **Closed** — Final status. The return is fully processed and closed out. Only resolved returns can be closed. Closed returns cannot be reopened or modified.

### How Returns Connect to Other Features

**Jobs:** Every return links to the original job that produced the returned goods via the OriginalJobId field. When a rework job is created, it links back to the return and also creates a RelatedTo job link between the original and rework jobs on the Kanban board.

**Customers:** Each return is associated with the customer who returned the goods. Returns can be filtered by customer to see all RMAs for a specific account.

**Sales Orders:** Returns are closely related to the sales order workflow. The returned goods were originally shipped against a sales order, and any replacement or credit may require a new sales order or adjustment.

**Rework Jobs:** When a return requires rework, the system can automatically create a new job with '[Rework]' prefixed to the original job title, assigned to the same track type and operator as the original. The rework job starts at the first stage of the production track and is set to High priority.

### When to Use Customer Returns

- When a customer contacts you about defective or incorrect goods
- When returned goods arrive at your facility and need to be logged
- When you need to track the inspection and resolution of quality issues
- When rework is required and you need to create a linked production job
- When you need an audit trail of all returns for a specific customer or job

### Access and Permissions

Customer returns are managed through the API by users with Admin, Manager, or Office Manager roles. The module is currently backend-only — there is no dedicated UI page. Returns are accessed via the `/api/v1/customer-returns` endpoints.", "sections": []}

title: Customer Returns — Process Walkthrough
summary: A conceptual walkthrough of the customer return process: receiving a return, inspection, rework decisions, resolution, and closing.
contentJson:
{"body": "## Customer Returns — Process Walkthrough

This walkthrough covers the end-to-end process for handling a customer return, from initial receipt through final closure. Since the Customer Returns module is currently backend-only (no dedicated UI page), this guide describes the conceptual workflow and the API operations involved at each step.

### Step 1: Receive the Return

When a customer contacts you about returning goods, the first step is to create a return record. This is done via `POST /api/v1/customer-returns` with the following information:

- **Customer** — Select the customer sending the goods back. The CustomerId links the return to their account.
- **Original Job** — Identify the job that produced the goods being returned. The OriginalJobId establishes traceability.
- **Reason** — Document why the goods are being returned (required, up to 1000 characters). Be specific: 'Parts out of tolerance on bore diameter' is better than 'Bad parts'.
- **Notes** — Any additional context (optional, up to 2000 characters). Include PO numbers, quantity returned, shipping details.
- **Return Date** — When the goods were or will be returned.
- **Create Rework Job** — If you already know rework is needed, set this to true. The system will automatically create a rework job linked to the original.

The system generates a unique RMA number (e.g., RMA-00001) and sets the initial status to Received. If a rework job was requested, the status starts at ReworkOrdered instead, and the rework job appears on the Kanban board.

### Step 2: Inspect the Returned Goods

Once the goods arrive at your facility, they need to be inspected. Update the return via `PUT /api/v1/customer-returns/{id}` to add inspection notes. The entity tracks:

- **InspectedById** — The user who performed the inspection
- **InspectedAt** — When the inspection occurred
- **InspectionNotes** — Detailed findings from the inspection (up to 2000 characters)

Document what you find: measurements, visual defects, comparison to specifications. This inspection record becomes part of the permanent audit trail.

### Step 3: Decide on Corrective Action

Based on the inspection, decide the corrective action:

- **Rework** — If the parts can be corrected, create a rework job (if not already created during Step 1). The rework job is a new production job with '[Rework]' in the title, linked to the same track type as the original, assigned High priority, and connected via a RelatedTo job link.
- **Replacement** — Create a new sales order and job to produce replacement parts.
- **Credit** — Issue a credit memo or adjust the invoice for the affected order.
- **No Action** — If the return is not justified (customer error, shipping damage covered by insurance), document this in the notes.

### Step 4: Resolve the Return

Once the corrective action is complete (rework finished, replacement shipped, credit issued), resolve the return via `POST /api/v1/customer-returns/{id}/resolve`. This moves the status from whatever current state it is in to Resolved.

**Important rules:**
- A return that is already Closed cannot be resolved (the system will reject the request)
- Resolving does not require the return to be in any specific prior status — it can go from Received, UnderInspection, or ReworkOrdered directly to Resolved

### Step 5: Close the Return

After the return is resolved and all follow-up is complete, close it via `POST /api/v1/customer-returns/{id}/close`. This is the final, irreversible status.

**Important rules:**
- Only returns in Resolved status can be closed
- Closed returns cannot be reopened, resolved again, or modified
- This ensures a clean audit trail — once closed, the record is final

### Filtering and Reviewing Returns

Use `GET /api/v1/customer-returns` to list all returns. The endpoint supports two filters:

- **customerId** — See all returns for a specific customer
- **status** — Filter by lifecycle stage (Received, UnderInspection, ReworkOrdered, Resolved, Closed)

Results are ordered by creation date (newest first). Each list item includes the RMA number, customer name, original job number, rework job number (if any), status, reason, return date, and creation timestamp.

### Best Practices

1. **Create returns promptly** — Log the RMA as soon as the customer reports the issue, even before the goods arrive back
2. **Be specific in reasons** — Vague reasons make it harder to identify patterns and improve quality
3. **Link rework jobs immediately** — If rework is likely, create the rework job during return creation to get it on the board faster
4. **Complete inspections thoroughly** — Inspection notes are permanent records used for quality trending
5. **Don't skip Resolve** — Always resolve before closing to maintain the audit trail
6. **Review returns by customer** — Periodic review of returns per customer helps identify recurring quality issues", "sections": []}

title: Customer Returns Knowledge Check
summary: Test your knowledge of the Customer Returns module: RMA lifecycle, statuses, rework jobs, API endpoints, and validation rules.
contentJson: {"questions": [{"id": "cr1", "text": "A customer calls to report defective parts from a recent order. What is the first step to handle this in Forge?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Create a new job on the Kanban board titled 'Rework' and assign it to production"}, {"id": "b", "text": "Create a customer return via POST /api/v1/customer-returns with the customer ID, original job ID, and reason for the return", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "Open the sales order and change its status to Returned"}, {"id": "d", "text": "Send the customer a credit memo and close the issue"}], "explanation": "The correct first step is to create a customer return record. This logs the RMA, generates a unique return number, links it to the original job and customer, and starts the return lifecycle at Received status."}, {"id": "cr2", "text": "What are the five statuses in the customer return lifecycle, in order?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Open, Inspecting, Reworking, Complete, Archived"}, {"id": "b", "text": "Received, UnderInspection, ReworkOrdered, Resolved, Closed", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "Created, Pending, InProgress, Done, Closed"}, {"id": "d", "text": "Submitted, Reviewed, Approved, Fulfilled, Closed"}], "explanation": "Customer returns follow the lifecycle: Received → UnderInspection → ReworkOrdered → Resolved → Closed. Not every return passes through all statuses — for example, a return may go from Received directly to Resolved if no inspection or rework is needed."}, {"id": "cr3", "text": "When you create a customer return with CreateReworkJob set to true, what happens automatically?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "The original job is moved back to its first production stage"}, {"id": "b", "text": "A new job is created with '[Rework]' prefix, High priority, same track type as the original, and a RelatedTo job link is established", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "The customer receives an automated email notification about the rework"}, {"id": "d", "text": "The return is automatically resolved since rework has been ordered"}], "explanation": "Setting CreateReworkJob to true creates a new job with '[Rework]' prefixed to the original title, High priority, same track type and assignee, starting at the first stage. A RelatedTo job link connects the original and rework jobs. The return status is set to ReworkOrdered."}, {"id": "cr4", "text": "You try to close a customer return that is in ReworkOrdered status. What happens?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "The return is closed successfully since all statuses can transition to Closed"}, {"id": "b", "text": "The system rejects the request because only Resolved returns can be closed", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "The return is closed but the rework job remains open on the Kanban board"}, {"id": "d", "text": "The system automatically resolves the return first, then closes it"}], "explanation": "Only returns in Resolved status can be closed. The system enforces this rule — attempting to close a non-resolved return throws an InvalidOperationException. You must first resolve the return via POST /{id}/resolve, then close it via POST /{id}/close."}, {"id": "cr5", "text": "What format does the system use for automatically generated RMA numbers?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "RET-YYYY-NNNN (year + sequence)"}, {"id": "b", "text": "RMA-XXXXX (e.g., RMA-00001), sequentially assigned and zero-padded to 5 digits", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "CR-NNN (simple 3-digit counter)"}, {"id": "d", "text": "The user manually enters an RMA number during creation"}], "explanation": "RMA numbers follow the format RMA-XXXXX (e.g., RMA-00001, RMA-00042). They are auto-generated by finding the highest existing number and incrementing by one, zero-padded to 5 digits. Users cannot manually set or change the RMA number."}, {"id": "cr6", "text": "Which roles have access to the customer returns endpoints?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "All authenticated users can view and manage returns"}, {"id": "b", "text": "Admin, Manager, and Office Manager only", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "Admin only — returns are a sensitive operation"}, {"id": "d", "text": "Admin, Manager, Office Manager, PM, and Engineer"}], "explanation": "The CustomerReturnsController is authorized for Admin, Manager, and Office Manager roles. Engineers and Production Workers do not have direct access to customer return management, though they may work on rework jobs that appear on the Kanban board."}, {"id": "cr7", "text": "You want to see all open returns for a specific customer. How do you query the API?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "GET /api/v1/customer-returns?customerId=42&status=Received to filter by customer and initial status", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "b", "text": "GET /api/v1/customers/42/returns — returns are nested under the customer endpoint"}, {"id": "c", "text": "GET /api/v1/customer-returns?search=CustomerName to search by name"}, {"id": "d", "text": "GET /api/v1/customer-returns and manually filter the response client-side"}], "explanation": "The GET /api/v1/customer-returns endpoint supports two query parameters: customerId (to filter by customer) and status (to filter by lifecycle stage). You can use both together. Results are returned ordered by creation date, newest first."}, {"id": "cr8", "text": "A return has been inspected and the inspector recorded their findings. Which fields on the entity capture this information?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "InspectorName, InspectionDate, and InspectionResult"}, {"id": "b", "text": "InspectedById, InspectedAt, and InspectionNotes", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "ReviewerId, ReviewDate, and ReviewComments"}, {"id": "d", "text": "Notes field only — there are no dedicated inspection fields"}], "explanation": "The CustomerReturn entity has three dedicated inspection fields: InspectedById (FK to the user who performed the inspection), InspectedAt (when the inspection occurred), and InspectionNotes (detailed findings, up to 2000 characters). These are separate from the general Notes field."}, {"id": "cr9", "text": "You attempt to resolve a customer return that has already been closed. What is the expected behavior?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "The return is moved back to Resolved status since resolution supersedes closure"}, {"id": "b", "text": "The system rejects the request because closed returns cannot be resolved", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "The return is duplicated as a new RMA with Resolved status"}, {"id": "d", "text": "Nothing happens — the resolve endpoint is idempotent on closed returns"}], "explanation": "The resolve handler explicitly checks: if the return's status is Closed, it throws an InvalidOperationException with 'Cannot resolve a closed return'. Closed is a final, irreversible status — no further status changes are permitted."}, {"id": "cr10", "text": "When a rework job is automatically created from a customer return, what description does the rework job receive?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "A copy of the original job's description"}, {"id": "b", "text": "'Rework for RMA {returnNumber}. Reason: {reason}' — including the RMA number and the return reason", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "'Rework required' with no additional context"}, {"id": "d", "text": "The description is left blank for the assignee to fill in"}], "explanation": "The auto-created rework job gets a description formatted as 'Rework for RMA {returnNumber}. Reason: {reason}'. For example: 'Rework for RMA RMA-00003. Reason: Parts out of tolerance on bore diameter'. This ensures anyone looking at the rework job on the Kanban board immediately understands its origin and purpose."}], "passingScore": 80, "shuffleOptions": true, "questionsPerQuiz": 8, "showExplanationsAfterSubmit": true}

title: Customers Overview
summary: What the Customers module does, the 9-tab detail page, contacts, addresses, credit management, and how customers connect to quotes, orders, jobs, and invoices.
contentJson:
{"body": "## Customers Overview

The Customers module is your central customer relationship hub. It tracks every customer your shop works with — their contact people, addresses, credit status, estimates, quotes, sales orders, jobs, invoices, and full activity history. Every customer gets a dedicated detail page with 10 tabs that give you a 360-degree view of the relationship.

### Customer List Page

The main `/customers` page shows all customers in a searchable, sortable data table. You can filter by active/inactive status and search by name, company, email, or phone. Click any row to navigate to that customer's detail page. The Add Customer button opens a quick-create dialog with name, company name, email, and phone fields.

### Customer Detail Page — 10 Tabs

Every customer detail page lives at `/customers/:id/:tab` and includes a header with the customer name, company, contact info, active/inactive status chip, and an accounting integration badge when synced with an external provider like QuickBooks.

#### Stats Bar

Below the header, a clickable stats bar shows live aggregates:
- **Open Estimates** — count of non-closed estimates
- **Open Quotes** — count of active quotes
- **Open Orders** — count of in-progress sales orders
- **Active Jobs** — count of jobs currently on the kanban board
- **Outstanding** — total dollar amount of unpaid invoices
- **YTD Revenue** — total revenue from this customer in the current year

Clicking a stat switches to the corresponding tab for quick drill-down.

#### Tab 1: Overview

Shows account details (name, company, email, phone, status, customer-since date), accounting integration info (provider, external ID, reference) when connected, and the Credit Status card.

**Credit Status Card** displays:
- Risk level chip (Low/Medium/High/OnHold)
- Credit Hold banner with reason and Release button (when on hold)
- Credit Limit, Open AR balance, Pending Orders total, Total Exposure, Available Credit, and Utilization percentage
- Visual utilization bar that turns red when over limit
- Place Hold button to block new orders with a required reason

#### Tab 2: Contacts

Manage contact people for this customer. Each contact is displayed as a card with avatar, name (Last, First format), role, email, phone, and primary contact badge. You can add, edit, and remove contacts. Contact roles come from reference data (database-driven, not hardcoded).

#### Tab 3: Interactions

Log and track customer interactions — calls, emails, meetings, and notes. Each interaction records the type, subject, contact, who logged it, date, duration, and notes. Filter by contact or type. The table shows type (with icon), subject, contact name, logged-by user, date, and duration.

#### Tab 4: Addresses

View all addresses on file. Each address card shows the address type chip (Billing, Shipping, etc.), a Default badge if applicable, and the full address (line 1, line 2, city, state, ZIP, country).

#### Tab 5: Estimates

Non-binding ballpark figures for potential work. Each estimate has a title, description, estimated amount, valid-until date, status (Draft, Sent, Accepted, Declined, Expired), and notes. Estimates can be converted to formal Quotes via the convert button. The table shows title, amount, status, valid-until date, created date, and action buttons (convert to quote, delete).

#### Tab 6: Quotes

Binding, line-itemized price commitments. The table shows quote number, status, total amount, expiration date, and created date. Click a row to open the full quote detail dialog.

#### Tab 7: Orders

Sales orders for this customer. The table shows order number, status (color-coded chip), total, requested date, and created date. Click to open the sales order detail dialog.

#### Tab 8: Jobs

All kanban jobs linked to this customer. The table shows job title, stage (color-coded chip matching the kanban stage color), due date, and created date. Click to open the job detail dialog.

#### Tab 9: Invoices

Invoices issued to this customer. The table shows invoice number, status, total, due date, and created date. Click to open the invoice detail dialog.

#### Tab 10: Activity

Chronological activity timeline showing all changes, comments, and events related to this customer. Uses the shared ActivityTimeline component.

### How Customers Connect to Other Features

**Quote-to-Cash Flow:** Customer → Estimate → Quote → Sales Order → Job → Shipment → Invoice → Payment. Every step in this chain links back to the customer record.

**Jobs:** When a job is created on the kanban board, it can be assigned to a customer. The customer's Jobs tab shows all linked jobs.

**Credit Management:** The credit status card monitors exposure (open AR + pending orders) against the credit limit. When a customer is on credit hold, new orders are blocked until the hold is released.

**Accounting Integration:** When connected to QuickBooks or another accounting provider, the customer record syncs bidirectionally. The provider badge and external IDs appear in the Overview tab.

### When to Use Customers

- When onboarding a new customer for the first time
- When looking up a customer's full history (orders, jobs, invoices)
- When checking credit status before accepting a new order
- When logging a phone call, email, or meeting with a customer contact
- When managing billing and shipping addresses
- When creating estimates or converting them to formal quotes", "sections": []}

title: Customers Knowledge Check

The Credit Status card is displayed on the Overview tab alongside account details and integration info."}, {"id": "cu9", "text": "You want to find all inactive customers who were created in the last year. How do you filter the customer list?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Set the Active filter to 'Inactive' — the table supports date column sorting to find recent entries", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "b", "text": "Run a search for 'inactive' in the search box"}, {"id": "c", "text": "Go to Reports and create a custom customer report"}, {"id": "d", "text": "You cannot filter inactive customers — they are hidden by default"}], "explanation": "The customer list page has an Active/Inactive filter dropdown. Set it to 'Inactive' to show only inactive customers, then sort by the Created column (date type) to find the most recent ones."}, {"id": "cu10", "text": "What information is displayed on a contact card in the Contacts tab?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Just the contact's name and email"}, {"id": "b", "text": "Avatar with initials, name (Last, First format), role, email (mailto link), phone (tel link), and Primary badge if applicable", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "Name, job title, department, and office location"}, {"id": "d", "text": "Name, email, and a list of their recent interactions"}], "explanation": "Contact cards show an avatar (with initials), the name in Last, First format, their role (from reference data), clickable email and phone links, and a blue 'Primary' chip badge if they are the primary contact."}, {"id": "cu11", "text": "A customer's credit utilization bar is red and shows 115%. What actions can you take?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Nothing — the system automatically blocks orders when over limit"}, {"id": "b", "text": "Place a credit hold via the Place Hold button, or increase the credit limit via the accounting system, or collect on outstanding invoices to reduce AR", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "Delete the oldest unpaid invoices to bring the balance down"}, {"id": "d", "text": "Archive the customer to prevent further orders"}], "explanation": "When over the credit limit, you can place a credit hold to formally block new orders (with a required reason), work with accounting to increase the credit limit, or focus on collecting outstanding invoices to reduce the AR balance and bring utilization back under 100%."}, {"id": "cu12", "text": "You want to share a direct link to a customer's Orders tab with a colleague. What URL format would you share?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "/customers?tab=orders&id=42"}, {"id": "b", "text": "/customers/42/orders", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "/customers/42?detail=orders"}, {"id": "d", "text": "/orders?customer=42"}], "explanation": "Customer detail pages use the URL pattern /customers/:id/:tab. Each tab is a route segment, so the Orders tab for customer 42 would be /customers/42/orders. This URL is bookmarkable and shareable."}, {"id": "cu13", "text": "What is the difference between an Estimate and a Quote in the customer detail?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "They are the same thing with different names"}, {"id": "b", "text": "Estimates are non-binding ballpark figures with a single amount; Quotes are binding, line-itemized commitments with per-item pricing", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "Estimates are for new customers; Quotes are for existing customers"}, {"id": "d", "text": "Estimates require approval; Quotes do not"}], "explanation": "Estimates are non-binding ballpark figures with a single estimated amount — quick and informal. Quotes are formal, binding commitments with individual line items (part, quantity, unit price). Estimates can be converted to Quotes when the customer is ready for a firm price."}, {"id": "cu14", "text": "You're filtering the Interactions tab and want to see only meetings with a specific contact. How?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Type 'meeting' in the search box"}, {"id": "b", "text": "Set the Contact filter to the specific contact and the Type filter to 'Meeting'", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "Sort the table by Type column and scroll to Meeting rows"}, {"id": "d", "text": "Interactions cannot be filtered by both contact and type simultaneously"}], "explanation": "The Interactions tab toolbar has two filter dropdowns: Contact (to filter by specific contact or '-- All Contacts --') and Type (to filter by Call, Email, Meeting, Note, or '-- All Types --'). Both filters can be used simultaneously."}, {"id": "cu15", "text": "When you archive a customer, what happens to their data?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "All data is permanently deleted including contacts, orders, and invoices"}, {"id": "b", "text": "The customer record is soft-deleted — all history is preserved but the customer won't appear in active views", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "Only the customer name is kept; all related records are purged"}, {"id": "d", "text": "The customer is moved to a separate Archive database"}], "explanation": "Archiving uses soft delete (sets DeletedAt timestamp). This follows the app-wide rule of never hard-deleting records."}], "passingScore": 80, "shuffleOptions": true, "questionsPerQuiz": 8, "showExplanationsAfterSubmit": true}

Description, Valid Until, and Notes are all optional."}, {"id": "est7", "text": "An estimate shows a yellow 'Expired' status chip. title: Dashboard Overview
summary: What the Dashboard shows, how widgets work, and how to customize your layout for daily productivity.
contentJson:
{"body": "## Dashboard Overview

The Dashboard is your home screen — the first thing you see after logging in. It gives you an at-a-glance summary of your day: tasks due, timers running, orders in progress, and planning cycle status. Everything is organized into draggable widgets that you can rearrange to match your workflow.

### Widget-Based Layout

The Dashboard uses a grid layout powered by gridstack. Each piece of information lives in its own widget card. You can drag widgets to rearrange them, and your layout is saved to your user preferences so it persists across sessions and devices.

### Core Widgets

**Today's Tasks:** Shows your assigned jobs sorted by due date. Overdue jobs are highlighted so you can see what needs immediate attention. Click any job to open its detail dialog.

**Active Timers:** If you have a running timer, this widget shows the elapsed time, a stop button, and a link to the job you're working on. When no timer is running, the widget shows a prompt to start one.

**Open Orders by Stage:** A summary of open orders grouped by stage — Quote, Production, QC, and Shipped counts. Gives you a quick sense of the pipeline without leaving the dashboard.

**Cycle Progress:** Shows your current planning cycle's completion percentage. The progress bar turns yellow when you're behind schedule, helping you prioritize.

**Mini Calendar:** A small calendar widget highlighting dates with due items. Click a date to see what's due.

**KPI Chips:** Compact metric displays showing key numbers (jobs completed this week, hours logged, etc.).

### Getting Started Banner

New users see a Getting Started banner that tracks onboarding progress — setting up your profile, completing compliance forms, reviewing training modules. The banner disappears once all onboarding steps are complete.

### Profile Completeness

For new users, a Profile Completeness widget shows which items are still missing: contact information, emergency contacts, compliance forms, etc. This helps ensure your account is fully set up.

### Loading and Performance

The Dashboard loads with a global loading overlay (LoadingService) because it fetches data from multiple endpoints simultaneously. Once all widgets have their data, the overlay clears and you see the full dashboard at once — no partial loading or flickering.", "sections": []}

title: EDI Overview
summary: What EDI is, the X12 and EDIFACT formats, how trading partners, transactions, and mappings work together in Forge.
contentJson:
{"body": "## EDI Overview

Electronic Data Interchange (EDI) lets your shop exchange structured business documents — purchase orders, invoices, shipping notices, and more — with trading partners electronically instead of through paper, email, or manual data entry.

Forge's EDI module lives under Admin > EDI and gives you a central place to manage trading partners, monitor inbound and outbound transactions, configure field mappings, and handle errors with retry support.

### What Is EDI?

EDI replaces manual document exchange with standardized electronic formats. When a customer sends you a purchase order via EDI, it arrives as a structured data file that Forge can parse, validate, and automatically apply — creating a Sales Order, Purchase Order, or other entity without anyone retyping the information.

The reverse also works: when you ship an order, Forge can generate an outbound EDI document (like an ASN or invoice) and send it to the trading partner automatically.

### EDI Formats

Forge supports two major EDI standards:

- **X12** — The dominant standard in North America. Transaction sets are identified by three-digit numbers: 850 (Purchase Order), 810 (Invoice), 856 (Advance Ship Notice), 855 (PO Acknowledgment), and many more.
- **EDIFACT** — The international standard used widely outside the US. Uses alphabetic message type codes like ORDERS, INVOIC, DESADV.

Each trading partner is configured with a default format, but individual transactions can use either format.

### Trading Partners

A trading partner is any company you exchange EDI documents with — typically customers or vendors. Each partner record stores:

- **EDI identifiers** — Qualifier ID and Qualifier Value that uniquely identify the partner in EDI envelopes. The Interchange Sender/Receiver IDs and Application Sender/Receiver IDs route documents correctly.
- **Format and transport** — Whether the partner uses X12 or EDIFACT, and how documents are delivered (AS2, SFTP, VAN, Email, API, or Manual).
- **Processing rules** — Whether inbound documents are auto-processed or held for manual review, and whether the partner requires functional acknowledgments (997/CONTRL).
- **Links** — A partner can be linked to an existing Customer or Vendor record in Forge for automatic entity matching.

### Transactions

Every EDI document exchange creates a transaction record. Transactions track:

- **Direction** — Inbound (received from partner) or Outbound (sent to partner).
- **Transaction Set** — The document type (e.g., 850 for a Purchase Order, 810 for an Invoice).
- **Control Numbers** — Interchange, Group, and Transaction control numbers that uniquely identify the document in the EDI envelope.
- **Status lifecycle** — Each transaction moves through a processing pipeline: Received → Parsing → Parsed → Validating → Validated → Processing → Applied (success) or Error/Rejected (failure).
- **Related entity** — Once processed, the transaction links to the Forge entity it created or updated (e.g., a Sales Order, PO, or Invoice).
- **Error handling** — Failed transactions show the error message and can be retried. The retry count and last retry timestamp are tracked.
- **Acknowledgments** — When a partner requires acknowledgments, the system tracks whether the 997/CONTRL was sent and links the acknowledgment transaction.

### Mappings

Mappings define how fields in an EDI document translate to fields in Forge entities. Each mapping belongs to a trading partner and a specific transaction set.

- **Field mappings** — Map EDI segment/element paths to Forge entity fields (e.g., PO1-07 → Part Number, N1-04 → Customer ID).
- **Value translations** — Convert partner-specific codes to Forge values (e.g., partner's item code '12345' maps to your Part Number 'WIDGET-001').
- **Default mapping** — One mapping per transaction set can be marked as default. When a document arrives, the system uses the default mapping unless overridden.

### Transport Methods

Forge supports multiple ways to send and receive EDI documents:

- **AS2** — The most common B2B protocol. Encrypted, signed, with built-in receipt (MDN) support.
- **SFTP** — Secure file transfer. Documents are placed in partner-specific directories.
- **VAN** — Value Added Network. A third-party service that routes documents between partners (e.g., SPS Commerce, TrueCommerce).
- **Email** — EDI documents sent as email attachments. Simple but less automated.
- **API** — Direct REST/SOAP API integration with the partner's system.
- **Manual** — Documents are uploaded/downloaded manually through the Forge UI. Good for low-volume partners or testing.

### Inbound Polling and Auto-Processing

When Auto Process is enabled on a trading partner, Forge automatically processes inbound documents through the full pipeline — parsing the EDI content, validating against the mapping rules, and applying the data to create or update entities. When Auto Process is off, inbound documents stop at the Received status for manual review before processing.

### When to Use EDI

- When a customer or vendor requires electronic document exchange
- When you want to eliminate manual data entry for recurring orders
- When you need an auditable trail of every document exchanged with a partner
- When your trading partner uses a VAN or AS2 and expects automated acknowledgments", "sections": []}

title: EDI — Guided Tour
summary: A guided tour of the Admin > EDI page: sub-tabs, trading partner table, partner dialog, transaction list, filters, transaction detail, and retry.
contentJson: {"steps": [{"element": ".edi-panel .tab-bar", "popover": {"side": "bottom", "title": "EDI Sub-Tabs", "description": "The EDI page has two sub-tabs: Trading Partners (manage your EDI partner configurations) and Transactions (monitor all inbound and outbound EDI document exchanges). Click a tab to switch views."}}, {"element": ".edi-panel__toolbar .action-btn--primary", "popover": {"side": "bottom", "title": "New Trading Partner", "description": "Click here to add a new trading partner. You'll enter the partner name, EDI qualifier IDs, default format (X12 or EDIFACT), transport method, and processing rules. The partner can optionally be linked to an existing Customer or Vendor."}}, {"element": "app-data-table[tableId='edi-partners']", "popover": {"side": "top", "title": "Trading Partners Table", "description": "Lists all configured trading partners. Columns show the partner name, linked customer/vendor, default format, transport method, transaction count, error count, and active status. Click column headers to sort. Each row has action buttons for testing the connection, editing, and deleting."}}, {"element": ".tab:nth-child(2)", "popover": {"side": "bottom", "title": "Transactions Tab", "description": "Switch to the Transactions tab to see all EDI document exchanges. title: EDI Knowledge Check
summary: Test your knowledge of the EDI module: formats, trading partners, transactions, mappings, statuses, and transport methods.
contentJson: {"questions": [{"id": "edi1", "text": "A new customer requires EDI document exchange for purchase orders. Where do you set this up in Forge?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Go to Customers, open the customer detail, and enable the EDI toggle"}, {"id": "b", "text": "Navigate to Admin > EDI, click New Trading Partner, and configure the partner with the customer's EDI identifiers, format, and transport method", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "Create a new integration in Admin > Integrations and select EDI as the type"}, {"id": "d", "text": "Go to Admin > Settings and add the customer's EDI credentials to the system settings"}], "explanation": "Trading partners are managed under Admin > EDI. Click New Trading Partner and fill in the name, qualifier ID/value, default format (X12 or EDIFACT), transport method, and processing rules. You can link the partner to an existing Customer record."}, {"id": "edi2", "text": "What is the difference between X12 and EDIFACT?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "X12 is for invoices only, EDIFACT is for purchase orders only"}, {"id": "b", "text": "X12 is the North American standard using numeric transaction set codes (like 850, 810); EDIFACT is the international standard using alphabetic message types (like ORDERS, INVOIC)", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "X12 is newer and replaces EDIFACT, which is deprecated"}, {"id": "d", "text": "X12 only works with AS2 transport, while EDIFACT only works with SFTP"}], "explanation": "X12 (ANSI ASC X12) is the dominant standard in North America, using 3-digit numeric codes for transaction sets (850=Purchase Order, 810=Invoice). EDIFACT (UN/EDIFACT) is the international standard using alphabetic codes (ORDERS, INVOIC). Both are actively used."}, {"id": "edi3", "text": "You see a transaction in the Transactions tab with status 'Error' (red chip). What action can you take?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Delete the transaction and ask the partner to resend"}, {"id": "b", "text": "Click the transaction row to view the error details, then click the Retry button to re-queue it for processing", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "Change the status manually to Applied to force it through"}, {"id": "d", "text": "Nothing — error transactions are permanently failed and must be handled outside Forge"}], "explanation": "Transactions in Error status show a Retry button (refresh icon) in the actions column. Click the row to see the error message and raw payload in the detail dialog, then click Retry to re-queue the transaction for processing. The retry count tracks how many attempts have been made."}, {"id": "edi4", "text": "What does the 'Auto Process' toggle on a trading partner control?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Whether outbound documents are sent automatically or queued for manual review"}, {"id": "b", "text": "Whether inbound documents are automatically parsed, validated, and applied, or held at Received status for manual review", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "Whether the partner's connection is tested automatically every hour"}, {"id": "d", "text": "Whether duplicate documents from this partner are automatically rejected"}], "explanation": "When Auto Process is ON (default), inbound EDI documents are automatically processed through the full pipeline: Received → Parsing → Parsed → Validating → Validated → Processing → Applied. When OFF, documents stop at the Received status and require manual action to proceed."}, {"id": "edi5", "text": "A trading partner's Qualifier ID is set to 'ZZ'. What does this mean?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "The partner uses a ZIP code as their identifier"}, {"id": "b", "text": "The identifier type is 'Mutually Defined' — the Qualifier Value is a code agreed upon by both parties", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "The partner has not been fully configured yet and 'ZZ' is a placeholder"}, {"id": "d", "text": "The partner uses a DUNS number for identification"}], "explanation": "'ZZ' is the EDI qualifier code for 'Mutually Defined,' meaning both trading partners have agreed on the format and meaning of the Qualifier Value. It is the most common qualifier when partners don't use a standard identifier like DUNS ('01') or UCC/EAN ('08'). It is also the default in Forge."}, {"id": "edi6", "text": "What is the purpose of an EDI mapping in Forge?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "It maps the network route between your server and the partner's server for document delivery"}, {"id": "b", "text": "It defines how fields in an EDI document translate to fields in Forge entities, and how partner-specific codes convert to your internal values", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "It creates a visual diagram of the EDI data flow for documentation purposes"}, {"id": "d", "text": "It maps user roles to EDI permissions so only authorized users can send documents"}], "explanation": "EDI mappings define two things: field mappings (which EDI segment/element maps to which Forge field, like PO1-07 → Part Number) and value translations (converting partner-specific codes to your internal values, like their item code '12345' to your part number 'WIDGET-001')."}, {"id": "edi7", "text": "You want to see only inbound transactions that failed processing. How do you filter the transaction list?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Set the Direction filter to Inbound and the Status filter to Error", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "b", "text": "Click the column header for Status and type 'Error' in the search box"}, {"id": "c", "text": "Use the search bar at the top and type 'Inbound Error'"}, {"id": "d", "text": "Switch to the Partners tab and look for partners with error counts > 0"}], "explanation": "The Transactions tab has two filter dropdowns at the top: Direction and Status. Set Direction to 'Inbound' and Status to 'Error' to see exactly the failed inbound transactions. The list refreshes immediately when you change either filter."}, {"id": "edi8", "text": "Which transport method provides built-in encryption, digital signatures, and delivery receipts (MDN)?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "SFTP"}, {"id": "b", "text": "Email"}, {"id": "c", "text": "AS2", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "d", "text": "VAN"}], "explanation": "AS2 (Applicability Statement 2) is the most common B2B EDI protocol. It uses HTTP/HTTPS with S/MIME encryption, digital signatures for authentication, and Message Disposition Notifications (MDN) to confirm delivery. It is the preferred transport for high-volume, security-sensitive EDI exchanges."}, {"id": "edi9", "text": "A trading partner has 'Require Acknowledgment' enabled. What happens when you receive an inbound document from them?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Forge sends a functional acknowledgment (997 for X12 or CONTRL for EDIFACT) back to the partner confirming receipt and processing status", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "b", "text": "Forge sends an email notification to the admin user that a document was received"}, {"id": "c", "text": "The transaction is held until a user manually clicks an Acknowledge button"}, {"id": "d", "text": "The partner's system automatically marks the document as delivered without any action from Forge"}], "explanation": "When Require Acknowledgment is ON, Forge automatically sends a functional acknowledgment — a 997 transaction set for X12 or a CONTRL message for EDIFACT — back to the trading partner. The acknowledgment transaction is linked to the original inbound transaction for traceability."}, {"id": "edi10", "text": "What does the 'Test Connection' button (wifi icon) do on a trading partner row?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Sends a test EDI document to the partner to verify end-to-end processing"}, {"id": "b", "text": "Sends a connectivity test to the partner's configured transport endpoint and reports success or failure via a snackbar message", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "Pings the partner's IP address to check if their server is online"}, {"id": "d", "text": "Toggles the partner between test mode and production mode"}], "explanation": "The Test Connection button sends a connectivity test to the partner's transport endpoint (AS2 URL, SFTP server, VAN mailbox, etc.). It shows a success snackbar if the connection succeeds or an error snackbar with the failure message if it doesn't. It does not send any actual EDI documents."}, {"id": "edi11", "text": "An EDI transaction shows a status of 'Acknowledged' (gray chip). What does this mean?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "The transaction was received but is waiting for a user to acknowledge and begin processing"}, {"id": "b", "text": "A functional acknowledgment (997/CONTRL) has been sent for this inbound transaction, confirming receipt to the trading partner", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "The related entity (e.g., Sales Order) has been acknowledged by the warehouse team"}, {"id": "d", "text": "The transaction failed but was acknowledged as a known issue that will not be retried"}], "explanation": "The Acknowledged status means a functional acknowledgment (997 for X12 or CONTRL for EDIFACT) was sent to the trading partner confirming that the inbound document was received and processed. The acknowledgment transaction is linked to the original for full traceability."}, {"id": "edi12", "text": "You need to set up EDI for a low-volume vendor who sends purchase orders occasionally. You are still configuring the integration. Which transport method is most appropriate to start with?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "AS2 — it is the industry standard and should be used from day one"}, {"id": "b", "text": "Manual — upload and download documents through the Forge UI while you configure and test the full integration", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "VAN — always use a VAN for low-volume partners to reduce complexity"}, {"id": "d", "text": "API — set up a direct REST API connection for maximum speed"}], "explanation": "Manual transport lets you upload and download EDI documents directly through the Forge UI. It is the best choice for initial setup, testing, and low-volume partners because it requires no external infrastructure. You can switch to AS2, SFTP, or a VAN later once the integration is validated."}], "passingScore": 80, "shuffleOptions": true, "questionsPerQuiz": 8, "showExplanationsAfterSubmit": true}

title: Estimates Overview
summary: What estimates are, how they differ from quotes, their non-binding nature, and how to convert them into formal quotes.
contentJson:
{"body": "## Estimates Overview

Estimates are non-binding ballpark figures you provide to customers before committing to a formal quote. They give customers a rough idea of cost without locking your shop into a fixed price. Estimates live inside the customer detail page on the Estimates tab — they are always tied to a specific customer.

### Estimates vs Quotes

Estimates and quotes serve different purposes in the sales pipeline:

- **Estimate** — A single dollar amount with a title and optional description. Non-binding. Not line-itemized. Think of it as 'here's roughly what this would cost.' Stored in the quotes table with type = Estimate.
- **Quote** — A binding, fixed-price commitment with individual line items (part, quantity, unit price). Line-itemized. Can be converted to a Sales Order. Stored in the quotes table with type = Quote.

Because estimates are intentionally simple, they have just one amount field — no line items, no tax calculations, no shipping addresses. This keeps the workflow fast when a customer calls and asks 'how much would it cost to make 500 of these?'

### The Estimate Lifecycle

Every estimate starts in **Draft** status and moves through a straightforward lifecycle:

1. **Draft** — Just created, still being refined. You can edit the title, amount, description, and notes freely.
2. **Sent** — You've shared the estimate with the customer (via email, PDF, or verbally). The estimate is still editable.
3. **Accepted** — The customer agreed to move forward based on the ballpark figure.
4. **Declined** — The customer decided not to proceed.
5. **Expired** — The estimate passed its Valid Until date without a decision.
6. **Converted to Quote** — The estimate was converted into a formal, line-itemized quote. The estimate record is preserved with a link to the generated quote.

### Converting to a Quote

When a customer accepts an estimate and wants to move forward, you convert it to a formal quote. Click the forward arrow icon on the estimate row and confirm. This:

- Creates a new Quote (type = Quote) in Draft status with an auto-generated quote number
- Carries over the customer, notes, and expiration date
- Sets the estimate's status to Converted to Quote
- Records the conversion timestamp
- Links the new quote back to the source estimate via the source_estimate_id field

The new quote starts empty (no line items) — you'll add parts, quantities, and pricing on the Quotes page. The estimate's single amount was just a ballpark; the quote is where you get precise.

### When to Use Estimates

- When a customer asks for a rough cost before you've done detailed engineering
- When you want to qualify a lead before investing time in a full quote
- When you need a fast turnaround on pricing (estimates take seconds to create)
- When the scope is still being defined and line items aren't known yet

### Where to Find Estimates

Estimates are accessed through the customer detail page. Navigate to Customers, select a customer, then click the Estimates tab. All estimates for that customer are listed in a sortable, filterable data table. You can create, edit, convert, and delete estimates directly from this tab.", "sections": []}

title: Events Overview
summary: What events are, the four event types, attendee management, RSVP workflow, and automatic reminders.
contentJson:
{"body": "## Events Overview

The Events module lets administrators and managers schedule and track company events — meetings, training sessions, safety briefings, and other gatherings. It lives under Admin > Events and provides a central calendar of everything happening in your organization.

### Event Types

Every event is categorized into one of four types:
- **Meeting** — team meetings, project reviews, stand-ups, 1-on-1s. Icon: groups.
- **Training** — skills training, onboarding sessions, certification prep. Icon: school.
- **Safety** — safety briefings, emergency drills, compliance reviews. Icon: health_and_safety.
- **Other** — company socials, announcements, facility tours, or anything that doesn't fit the above. Icon: event.

The type determines the icon shown in the events table and helps with filtering.

### Attendees and RSVP

When creating or editing an event, you select attendees from the list of active employees. Each attendee receives a notification inviting them to the event. Attendees can then respond with one of these statuses:
- **Invited** — default status, no response yet
- **Accepted** — attendee confirmed they will attend
- **Declined** — attendee cannot attend
- **Attended** — post-event confirmation that the person was present (useful for compliance tracking)

The attendee count is shown in the events table so you can see at a glance how many people are invited to each event.

### Required vs Optional Events

Events can be marked as Required Attendance. When toggled on:
- The invitation notification is sent with warning severity instead of info
- The events table shows a yellow Required chip next to the event
- This signals to employees and managers that attendance is mandatory (e.g., safety briefings, compliance training)

Optional events show as plain text in the table.

### Automatic Reminders

The system runs a background job that sends reminder notifications 15 minutes before an event starts. This ensures attendees don't miss upcoming events, even if they didn't check the original invitation. The reminder is sent once — the ReminderSentAt timestamp on the event prevents duplicate notifications.

### Where Events Appear

- **Admin > Events** — the main management view where admins create, edit, filter, and cancel events
- **Shop Floor Display** — upcoming events appear in the shop floor's upcoming section so production workers see what's scheduled
- **Employee Detail** — each employee's profile has an Events tab showing events they're invited to or have attended

### Cancelling Events

Events are soft-cancelled, not deleted. When you cancel an event, the IsCancelled flag is set and the event no longer appears in the default list view. Cancelled events are excluded from the events query, keeping the table clean while preserving the historical record.

### When to Use Events

- Scheduling a team meeting or project review
- Organizing mandatory safety briefings or emergency drills
- Planning training sessions for new hires or skill development
- Tracking attendance for compliance purposes (who attended which safety training)
- Announcing company-wide gatherings or social events", "sections": []}

title: Navigating the App
summary: A comprehensive overview of the sidebar, header, notifications, search, theme switching, and navigation structure.
contentJson:
{"body": "## Navigating the App

Forge has a consistent layout across every page: a collapsible sidebar on the left, a fixed header bar at the top, and a scrollable content area in the center.

### Sidebar Navigation

The sidebar is your primary navigation tool. In its collapsed state, it shows only icons (52px wide). Hover or click the expand toggle to see full labels (200px wide). Your sidebar state is saved to your preferences — it persists across sessions and devices.

**Sidebar sections:**
- **Dashboard** — your daily home page with widgets
- **Board** — the kanban board for active jobs
- **Backlog** — all jobs not on the board
- **Planning** — planning cycle management
- **Parts** — parts catalog with BOMs and process steps
- **Inventory** — stock levels, bins, movements
- **Customers** — CRM with contacts and addresses
- **Vendors** — vendor management and PO history
- **Purchase Orders** — PO creation and receiving
- **Sales Orders** — order management
- **Quotes** — price quoting
- **Shipments** — shipping and tracking
- **Invoices** — billing (standalone mode)
- **Payments** — payment recording (standalone mode)
- **Leads** — sales pipeline
- **Expenses** — expense submission and approval
- **Assets** — equipment and tooling register
- **Time Tracking** — timers and manual entries
- **Quality** — QC templates and inspections
- **Reports** — report builder and saved reports
- **Calendar** — job due dates and PO deliveries
- **Chat** — internal messaging
- **AI** — AI assistant
- **Training** — learning modules and paths
- **Admin** — system configuration (admin only)
- **Account** — your profile, compliance, security

### Header Bar

The 44px header bar stays fixed at the top of every page. It contains:
- **Search icon** — opens global search (searches jobs, parts, customers, vendors, and more)
- **Notification bell** — shows unread count badge; click to open the notification panel
- **Chat icon** — opens the chat panel; shows unread message count
- **User avatar** — opens the user menu with theme toggle, account link, and logout

### Theme Switching

Click your avatar in the top-right and toggle between Light and Dark themes. Your choice is saved to localStorage and syncs across tabs immediately via the storage event. Dark theme swaps all CSS custom properties (--bg, --surface, --text, etc.).

### Keyboard Shortcuts

Press **?** (question mark) anywhere to see available keyboard shortcuts. Common shortcuts include:
- **/** — focus global search
- **Ctrl+K** — open command palette
- **Escape** — close current dialog or panel

### URL-Based Navigation

Every significant view state is reflected in the URL. This means:
- You can bookmark any page, tab, or filtered view
- Browser back/forward buttons work naturally
- Sharing a URL puts the recipient on the exact same view
- Refreshing the page preserves your state", "sections": []}

title: Expenses Overview
summary: What the Expenses module does: submission flow, approval workflow, status tracking, and manager review.
contentJson:
{"body": "## Expenses Overview

The Expenses module lets you submit, track, and manage company expenses in a single searchable table. Managers can approve or reject pending expenses directly from the table.

### Submission Flow

To submit an expense:
1. Navigate to /expenses.
2. Click the **Add Expense** button in the page header.
3. Fill in Amount (required, min $0.01), Date (required), and Category (required — loaded from reference data configured in Admin).
4. Optionally add a Description.
5. Click **Submit**. The expense enters **Pending** status.

Once submitted, the expense appears in the main table for everyone to see.

### Approval Workflow

Managers and Admins can approve or reject pending expenses directly in the table. Each pending expense row shows two inline action buttons:
- **Approve** (checkmark icon) — immediately marks the expense as Approved (green chip).
- **Reject** (X icon) — immediately marks the expense as Rejected (red chip).

These action buttons only appear for expenses with Pending status.

### Filtering and Search

The page header includes:
- A **Search** input that filters by description, category, or submitter name.
- A **Status** filter dropdown with options: All, Pending, Approved, Rejected, SelfApproved.
- A **Total Amount** display showing the sum of currently visible expenses.

### Expense Statuses

- **Pending** (warning/yellow chip) — Awaiting manager approval.
- **Approved** (success/green chip) — Reviewed and approved.
- **Rejected** (error/red chip) — Reviewed and rejected.
- **SelfApproved** (success/green chip) — Submitted by a manager with auto-approve privileges.

### Draft Auto-Save

The expense dialog supports draft auto-save. If you accidentally close the dialog before saving, your form data is preserved in IndexedDB and restored when you reopen the dialog.", "sections": []}

Form data is stored in IndexedDB and automatically recovered when you reopen the dialog."}, {"id": "ex7", "text": "How can you filter the expenses table to see only rejected expenses?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Type 'rejected' in the search input"}, {"id": "b", "text": "Use the Status filter dropdown and select 'Rejected'", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "Click the Rejected column header to sort by rejections"}, {"id": "d", "text": "Navigate to a separate Rejected Expenses page"}], "explanation": "The Status filter dropdown in the page header lets you select a specific status: All, Pending, Approved, Rejected, or SelfApproved. You cannot submit an expense for $0.00 or a negative amount."}, {"id": "ex12", "text": "Which of the following is NOT a column in the Expenses data table?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Category"}, {"id": "b", "text": "Submitted By"}, {"id": "c", "text": "Vendor", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "d", "text": "Job"}], "explanation": "The Expenses table columns are: Date, Category, Description, Job, Submitted By, Amount, Status, and Actions. There is no Vendor column."}, {"id": "ex13", "text": "When are the inline approve/reject action buttons visible on an expense row?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Always — on every expense regardless of status"}, {"id": "b", "text": "Only for expenses with Pending status", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "Only for expenses submitted by other users"}, {"id": "d", "text": "Only for expenses over $100"}], "explanation": "The approve (checkmark) and reject (X) inline action buttons only appear for expenses that are in Pending status. Once approved or rejected, the buttons are hidden."}, {"id": "ex14", "text": "What color chip does each expense status display?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Pending: blue, Approved: green, Rejected: red, SelfApproved: gray"}, {"id": "b", "text": "Pending: yellow, Approved: green, Rejected: red, SelfApproved: green", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "Pending: gray, Approved: blue, Rejected: yellow, SelfApproved: green"}, {"id": "d", "text": "All statuses use the same neutral gray chip"}], "explanation": "Pending uses a warning/yellow chip, Approved and SelfApproved both use success/green chips, and Rejected uses an error/red chip."}, {"id": "ex15", "text": "What happens when you hover over a disabled Submit button in the Create Expense dialog?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Nothing happens — the button is just grayed out"}, {"id": "b", "text": "A tooltip says 'Please fill in all fields'"}, {"id": "c", "text": "A validation popover lists which specific fields need attention", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "d", "text": "The invalid fields flash red to draw your attention"}], "explanation": "When the form is invalid, hovering over the Save/Submit button displays a validation popover that lists each field violation — for example, 'Amount is required' or 'Category is required'."}], "passingScore": 80, "shuffleOptions": true, "questionsPerQuiz": 8, "showExplanationsAfterSubmit": true}

title: Inventory Overview
summary: What the Inventory module does: stock tracking, bin locations, movements, receiving from POs, reservations, cycle counts, and replenishment.
contentJson:
{"body": "## Inventory Overview

The Inventory module tracks every piece of material and finished goods in your facility — where it is, how much you have, and how it got there. It is the central hub for stock visibility, receiving, transfers, adjustments, and replenishment planning.

### Core Concepts

**Storage Locations** form a hierarchy: Area → Rack → Shelf → Bin. An Area might be 'Warehouse A', a Rack is a physical rack within that area, a Shelf is a level on the rack, and a Bin is a specific slot. Every piece of stock lives in a Bin — the lowest level of the hierarchy. Each location can have a barcode for quick scanning.

**Bin Content** represents what is physically stored in a bin. Each bin content record tracks the entity type (part, production run, assembly, or tooling), quantity, lot number, status (Stored, Reserved, Ready to Ship, or QC Hold), and who placed it there. A single bin can hold multiple items.

**Bin Movements** record every stock movement with full traceability. Each movement captures the item, quantity, source location, destination location, the person who moved it, and the reason (Receive, Pick, Restock, QC Release, Ship, Move, Adjustment, Return, Transfer, or Cycle Count).

### How Inventory Connects to Other Features

**Purchase Orders:** When materials arrive from a vendor, the Receiving tab lets you receive against open PO lines. Select the PO line, enter the quantity received, assign a storage location and optional lot number. This creates a bin content record and a movement record with reason 'Receive'.

**Parts Catalog:** The Stock tab shows inventory summarized by part — total on hand, reserved quantity, and available quantity. Expanding a row reveals the bin-level breakdown showing exactly where each part is stored.

**Jobs and Production:** Stock can be reserved for specific jobs. Reservations reduce the available quantity without moving the physical stock. When production picks the material, the reservation is released and a movement with reason 'Pick' is recorded.

**Quality Control:** Received goods can be placed on QC Hold status, preventing them from being picked or shipped until quality inspection is complete. QC Release changes the status back to Stored and records a movement.

**Shipping:** When orders ship, inventory is decremented with a 'Ship' movement reason. Stock marked as 'Ready to Ship' is staged for outbound shipments.

### Tabs at a Glance

- **Stock** — Part-level inventory summary with expandable bin details. Search by part number. Low stock alerts highlight parts below minimum thresholds.
- **Locations** — Hierarchical tree view of all storage locations. Create, expand, and browse the Area → Rack → Shelf → Bin structure. Select a bin to see its contents.
- **Movements** — Chronological log of every stock movement. Shows item, quantity, from/to locations, reason, who moved it, and when.
- **Receiving** — History of goods received against purchase orders. Receive new stock by selecting an open PO line.
- **Stock Ops** — Transfer stock between bins or adjust quantities with a reason. Two dialogs: Transfer (move stock from one bin to another) and Adjust (correct a quantity with a mandatory reason).
- **Cycle Counts** — Create and review cycle counts for specific locations. Each count captures expected vs. actual quantities and calculates variance. Counts can be Pending, Approved, or Rejected.
- **Reservations** — View and manage stock reservations tied to jobs or sales orders. Create new reservations or release existing ones.
- **Replenishment** — Burn rate analysis and reorder suggestions. Shows 30/60/90-day consumption rates, days of stock remaining, projected stockout dates, and auto-generated reorder suggestions that can be approved into purchase orders.
- **UOM** — Unit of measure management and conversions.

### Barcode Scanning

The Inventory module is scanner-aware. When a barcode is scanned on this page, it automatically populates the search field and switches to the Stock tab. Storage locations can also have barcodes assigned for quick identification during receiving and picking.", "sections": []}
