Scans on this page are routed to the parts search filter."}, {"label": "Scan behavior", "value": "When a barcode is scanned, the scanned value is placed into the search field and the parts list is reloaded with the scanned value as the search term. This finds the part by its barcode-encoded part number."}, {"label": "Supported scanners", "value": "USB barcode scanners (keyboard wedge mode) and NFC readers (ACR122U with NTAG215 tags). Scanner must be in keyboard emulation mode."}], "heading": "Barcode Scanner Integration"}]}

title: Parts Catalog Knowledge Check
summary: Test your knowledge of the Parts Catalog: types, statuses, BOM lines, source types, operations, routing, inventory thresholds, and pricing.
contentJson: {"questions": [{"id": "pt1", "text": "You need to create a new part that is built from multiple sub-components. What part type should you select?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Part"}, {"id": "b", "text": "Assembly", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "RawMaterial"}, {"id": "d", "text": "Consumable"}], "explanation": "Assembly is the correct type for a part that is built from other parts. After creating the Assembly, you'll add BOM lines listing each sub-component and its quantity."}, {"id": "pt2", "text": "A BOM line has a source type of 'Make'. What does this mean?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "The component is purchased from an external vendor"}, {"id": "b", "text": "The component is pulled from existing inventory stock"}, {"id": "c", "text": "The component is manufactured in-house, which may trigger a sub-job", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "d", "text": "The component is a raw material that requires no processing"}], "explanation": "Source type 'Make' means the component is manufactured in-house. During production planning, this can trigger the creation of a sub-job to produce the component. 'Buy' is for purchased items and 'Stock' is for pulling from inventory."}, {"id": "pt3", "text": "You want to track how much cutting fluid is consumed during production but it isn't part of the finished product. What part type is appropriate?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "RawMaterial"}, {"id": "b", "text": "Part"}, {"id": "c", "text": "Consumable", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "d", "text": "Packaging"}], "explanation": "Consumable is the correct type for items used during manufacturing but not part of the finished product. Examples include cutting fluid, sandpaper, welding gas, and gloves."}, {"id": "pt4", "text": "A part's status is set to 'Obsolete'. What happens to the part record?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "The record is permanently deleted from the database"}, {"id": "b", "text": "The record is soft-deleted and hidden from all views including search"}, {"id": "c", "text": "The record is preserved for historical traceability but won't appear in active searches or new orders", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "d", "text": "The record is moved to an archive table and can only be viewed by admins"}], "explanation": "Obsolete parts are never deleted. The full record (BOM, operations, pricing history, files) is preserved for traceability — past jobs and old BOMs still reference it. The part simply won't appear in active filtered views or be available for new orders."}, {"id": "pt5", "text": "You need to add a BOM line for a purchased component that takes 14 days to arrive. Which fields should you fill in?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Child Part, Quantity, Source Type = Buy, and Lead Time Days = 14", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "b", "text": "Child Part, Quantity, and Reference Designator only"}, {"id": "c", "text": "Child Part, Source Type = Stock, and Lead Time Days = 14"}, {"id": "d", "text": "Child Part, Quantity, Source Type = Make, and Notes = '14 day lead time'"}], "explanation": "For a purchased component, select the child part, set the quantity needed, choose Source Type = Buy (indicating it comes from a vendor), and enter Lead Time Days = 14 to record the procurement timeline. Lead Time Days is a structured field used for planning — don't put it in notes."}, {"id": "pt6", "text": "What is the purpose of the Reference Designator field on a BOM line?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "It stores the vendor's catalog number for the component"}, {"id": "b", "text": "It identifies where the component goes in the assembly, using engineering references like R1, C4, or J2", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "It links the BOM line to an operation in the routing"}, {"id": "d", "text": "It sets the sort order of the BOM line in the table"}], "explanation": "Reference Designator is an engineering reference that identifies where a component goes in the assembly. It's commonly used in electronics (R1 for resistor 1, C4 for capacitor 4, J2 for connector 2) but can be used for any assembly where component placement matters."}, {"id": "pt7", "text": "An operation in the routing has the QC Checkpoint toggle turned ON. What does this mean?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "The operation is skipped during production and only performed during quality audits"}, {"id": "b", "text": "This step requires a quality inspection before the next production step can proceed", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "The operation automatically generates a QC report when completed"}, {"id": "d", "text": "Only quality team members can perform this operation"}], "explanation": "When QC Checkpoint is enabled on an operation, it means a quality inspection is required at this step before production can move to the next operation. The QC Criteria field describes what to inspect — dimensions, surface finish, tolerances, functional tests, etc."}, {"id": "pt8", "text": "You want to see which parent assemblies use a specific part as a component. Where do you look?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "The BOM tab on the part's detail dialog"}, {"id": "b", "text": "The Used In tab on the part's detail dialog", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "The Routing tab on the part's detail dialog"}, {"id": "d", "text": "The Reports module with a BOM explosion report"}], "explanation": "The Used In tab shows the reverse BOM relationship — all parent assemblies that include this part as a component. It displays a data table with parent part number, description, and quantity. Click a row to navigate directly to the parent part's detail."}, {"id": "pt9", "text": "A part's inventory quantity drops below the Min Stock Threshold. What visual indicator appears?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "The part row turns red in the parts table"}, {"id": "b", "text": "A yellow warning icon and 'Low Stock' label appear in the part detail panel's inventory summary", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "An email notification is sent to the purchasing team"}, {"id": "d", "text": "The part's status automatically changes to Draft"}], "explanation": "When inventory drops below the Min Stock Threshold, the part detail panel's inventory summary section shows a yellow warning icon and 'Low Stock' text next to the total quantity. The part status is not changed automatically — that's a manual action."}, {"id": "pt10", "text": "What is the difference between the BOM table view and the BOM tree view?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Table view shows only top-level components; tree view shows all levels"}, {"id": "b", "text": "Table view is a flat sortable data table with column filtering; tree view shows hierarchical nested sub-assemblies visually", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "Table view is read-only; tree view allows editing entries inline"}, {"id": "d", "text": "There is no difference — they show the same information in the same format"}], "explanation": "Table view presents BOM lines as a flat data table with sortable columns, per-column filtering, and action buttons. Tree view shows the same entries in a hierarchical visualization, making it easy to see nested sub-assemblies and their components at a glance."}, {"id": "pt11", "text": "You want to assign BOM materials to a specific manufacturing step. How do you do this?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Drag BOM lines from the BOM tab onto the operation in the Routing tab"}, {"id": "b", "text": "Open the operation dialog, go to the Materials tab, and select from available BOM lines", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "Edit the BOM line and set a 'Consumed At Step' field"}, {"id": "d", "text": "Materials are automatically assigned to operations based on the step number"}], "explanation": "Open the operation dialog (click the operation card in the Routing tab), switch to the Materials tab, and select from BOM lines that haven't been assigned to other operations. Specify the quantity consumed at this step. This connects the BOM to the routing at the operation level."}, {"id": "pt12", "text": "What file types can be uploaded to a part's Files tab?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Only PDF and image files"}, {"id": "b", "text": "PDF, CAD files (STEP, STL, DXF, DWG), and images (PNG, JPG, JPEG), up to 50MB each", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "Any file type with no size limit"}, {"id": "d", "text": "Only STL files for 3D viewing"}], "explanation": "The Files tab accepts .pdf, .step, .stl, .dxf, .dwg, .png, .jpg, and .jpeg files with a maximum size of 50MB per file. STL files get special treatment — when one is uploaded, a 3D Viewer tab appears allowing interactive Three.js rendering of the part."}, {"id": "pt13", "text": "You scan a barcode on the Parts page. What happens?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "A new part is created with the scanned value as the part number"}, {"id": "b", "text": "The scanned value is placed into the search field and the parts list reloads to find the matching part", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "The scanned part's detail dialog opens immediately"}, {"id": "d", "text": "Nothing — barcode scanning is only supported on the Inventory page"}], "explanation": "On the Parts page, the scanner context is set to 'parts'. When a barcode is scanned, the scanned value is automatically placed into the search field and the parts list reloads with that value as the search term. This finds the part by its barcode-encoded part number."}, {"id": "pt14", "text": "How do you set a new list price for a part?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Edit the part and change the price field in the edit dialog"}, {"id": "b", "text": "In the part detail Info tab, fill in the New Price, Effective From date, and optional notes, then click Set Price", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "Go to Admin → Price Lists and update the price there"}, {"id": "d", "text": "Prices can only be set through the accounting integration"}], "explanation": "Pricing is managed directly in the part detail panel's Info tab. Enter the new unit price, select the effective date (defaults to today), add optional notes, and click Set Price. The previous price automatically becomes historical and appears in the price history table below."}, {"id": "pt15", "text": "A part of type Tooling can be linked to which other module?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "The Kanban Board — tooling parts appear as cards on a dedicated Tooling board"}, {"id": "b", "text": "The Assets module — linked to a tooling asset for shot count tracking and maintenance scheduling", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "The Vendors module — tooling parts are automatically linked to their manufacturer"}, {"id": "d", "text": "The Time Tracking module — tooling usage is tracked as time entries"}], "explanation": "Tooling-type parts can be linked to a tooling asset in the Assets module via the Tooling Asset entity picker on the part form. This connects the part to the asset's shot count tracking and maintenance schedule, giving full traceability from the part back to the physical tool that produces it."}], "passingScore": 80, "shuffleOptions": true, "questionsPerQuiz": 8, "showExplanationsAfterSubmit": true}

title: Payments Overview
summary: What the Payments module does, standalone vs integrated mode, payment methods, and how payments apply to invoices.
contentJson:
{"body": "## Payments Overview

The Payments module records incoming customer payments and applies them to outstanding invoices. It is part of the accounting boundary — meaning it operates in **standalone mode** when no external accounting provider (QuickBooks, Xero, etc.) is connected. When an accounting provider is integrated, payments sync from the external system and the module becomes read-only.

### Standalone vs Integrated Mode

**Standalone mode (no accounting provider):** You have full control. Create payments, apply them to invoices, delete unapplied payments, and track payment history directly in Forge. This is the default for shops that don't use an external accounting system.

**Integrated mode (accounting provider connected):** A blue banner appears at the top of the Payments page indicating that payments are managed by the connected provider (e.g., QuickBooks Online). Payment data syncs automatically — you can view payments but cannot create, edit, or delete them from Forge. All payment operations happen in the accounting system.

### Payment Methods

Every payment records how it was received. The supported methods are:
- **Cash** — physical currency received
- **Check** — paper check (use the Reference Number field to record the check number)
- **Credit Card** — card payment (reference number can hold the last four digits or transaction ID)
- **Bank Transfer** — ACH or direct bank transfer
- **Wire** — wire transfer (reference number holds the wire confirmation)
- **Other** — any method not covered above

### Applying Payments to Invoices

A payment can be applied to one or more invoices. Each application specifies which invoice receives how much of the payment. This enables:

- **Full payment** — the entire payment amount covers one invoice exactly
- **Partial payment** — only part of the payment goes to an invoice (the invoice moves to Partially Paid status)
- **Split payment** — one payment covers portions of multiple invoices
- **Unapplied payment** — a payment (or portion of it) is recorded without being tied to any invoice yet. The unapplied amount shows in the table and is highlighted in the detail view.

When a payment application fully covers an invoice's remaining balance, that invoice automatically moves to Paid status. If a partial amount is applied, the invoice moves to Partially Paid.

### Validation Rules

The total of all applications on a payment cannot exceed the payment amount. Each individual application amount must be positive, and it cannot exceed the remaining balance on its target invoice.

### When to Use Payments

- When you receive a check, cash, or electronic payment from a customer
- When you need to record a partial payment against a large invoice
- When a single customer payment covers multiple invoices
- When you need to track unapplied payments (money received before invoicing)
- When reviewing payment history for a customer via the detail dialog", "sections": []}

title: Payments Knowledge Check
summary: Test your knowledge of the Payments module: methods, invoice applications, standalone vs integrated mode, validation, and common workflows.
contentJson: {"questions": [{"id": "pmt1", "text": "A customer mails you a check for $5,000 that covers two invoices. How do you record this in Forge?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Create two separate payments of $2,500 each, one for each invoice"}, {"id": "b", "text": "Click Record Payment, enter $5,000 with method Check, then add two invoice applications splitting the amount across both invoices", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "Go to each invoice and mark it as Paid, then create a payment for the total"}, {"id": "d", "text": "Create one payment and leave it unapplied — the system will match invoices automatically"}], "explanation": "A single payment can be applied to multiple invoices. Create one payment for $5,000, select Check as the method, then use the Invoice Applications section to add each invoice with its respective portion of the payment."}, {"id": "pmt2", "text": "You see a yellow 'Unapplied' amount of $1,200 on a payment row in the table. What does this mean?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "The payment failed to process and $1,200 was returned"}, {"id": "b", "text": "$1,200 of the payment has not been applied to any invoice yet", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "The customer still owes $1,200 on the payment"}, {"id": "d", "text": "$1,200 was applied to an invoice that was later voided"}], "explanation": "Unapplied amount means that portion of the payment has not been linked to any invoice. The yellow highlight draws attention to money received but not yet accounted for against specific invoices."}, {"id": "pmt3", "text": "What happens to an invoice when a payment application fully covers its remaining balance?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "The invoice is deleted from the system"}, {"id": "b", "text": "The invoice status automatically changes to Paid", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "Nothing — you must manually mark the invoice as Paid"}, {"id": "d", "text": "The invoice is archived and hidden from the list"}], "explanation": "When a payment application covers an invoice's full remaining balance, the system automatically sets the invoice status to Paid. For partial applications, the invoice moves to Partially Paid."}, {"id": "pmt4", "text": "Your shop just connected QuickBooks Online. What happens to the Payments page?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Nothing changes — payments are always managed locally"}, {"id": "b", "text": "A blue banner appears indicating payments are managed by QuickBooks, the Record Payment button is hidden, and data becomes read-only", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "All existing payment records are deleted and replaced with QuickBooks data"}, {"id": "d", "text": "The Payments page is completely removed from the navigation"}], "explanation": "Payments is an accounting-bounded feature. When an accounting provider is connected, the module switches to read-only mode with a banner explaining that payments are managed by the provider. Existing data is preserved, and new data syncs from QuickBooks."}, {"id": "pmt5", "text": "You want to delete a payment, but the Delete button is not visible in the detail panel. Why?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "You don't have the Admin role required to delete payments"}, {"id": "b", "text": "The payment has one or more invoice applications — you must remove all applications before deleting", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "Payments can never be deleted, only archived"}, {"id": "d", "text": "The payment was synced from an accounting provider and cannot be modified locally"}], "explanation": "A payment can only be deleted when it has zero invoice applications. This prevents orphaned invoice status changes. Remove all applications first, then the Delete button will appear."}, {"id": "pmt6", "text": "A customer pays $3,000 by wire transfer, but you haven't created the invoice yet. How should you handle this?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Wait until the invoice is created before recording the payment"}, {"id": "b", "text": "Record the payment with method Wire and $3,000 amount, but don't add any invoice applications — the unapplied amount will track the unmatched funds", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "Create a placeholder invoice for $3,000, then immediately apply the payment to it"}, {"id": "d", "text": "Record it as a Note on the customer record instead of a payment"}], "explanation": "You can record a payment without applying it to any invoice. The full amount shows as Unapplied (highlighted yellow in the table), making it easy to find and apply later once the invoice is created."}, {"id": "pmt7", "text": "What is the purpose of the Reference Number field on a payment?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "It is the auto-generated payment number used as the primary identifier"}, {"id": "b", "text": "It records an external reference like a check number, wire confirmation, or transaction ID", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "It links the payment to a sales order number"}, {"id": "d", "text": "It is required for all payment methods except Cash"}], "explanation": "Reference Number is an optional field for recording external identifiers — check numbers, wire confirmations, credit card transaction IDs, or ACH references. It's searchable from the payments list. The auto-generated Payment # is a separate field."}, {"id": "pmt8", "text": "You try to apply $2,000 of a payment to an invoice that only has a $1,500 remaining balance. What happens?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "The system accepts it and creates a $500 credit on the invoice"}, {"id": "b", "text": "The system rejects it — the application amount cannot exceed the invoice's remaining balance", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "The system applies $1,500 and leaves $500 unapplied automatically"}, {"id": "d", "text": "The system accepts it but flags the invoice as overpaid"}], "explanation": "The server validates that each application amount does not exceed the target invoice's remaining balance. If the amount is too high, the request is rejected with an error message showing the invoice number and its actual balance."}, {"id": "pmt9", "text": "Which of the following is NOT a supported payment method in Forge?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Bank Transfer"}, {"id": "b", "text": "Wire"}, {"id": "c", "text": "PayPal", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "d", "text": "Other"}], "explanation": "The six supported payment methods are Cash, Check, Credit Card, Bank Transfer, Wire, and Other. PayPal or any non-listed method should be recorded using the Other method, with details in the Notes field."}, {"id": "pmt10", "text": "You need to find all Check payments from a specific customer. What's the fastest approach?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Export the payments to CSV and filter in Excel"}, {"id": "b", "text": "Type the customer name in the Search box and select Check from the Method filter dropdown", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "Go to the customer detail page and look at their payment history tab"}, {"id": "d", "text": "Use the Reports module to build a custom payment report"}], "explanation": "The Payments list page has both a text search (filters by payment number, customer name, or reference number) and a Method filter dropdown. Combining these two filters — searching for the customer name and selecting Check — gives you the exact results you need instantly."}], "passingScore": 80, "shuffleOptions": true, "questionsPerQuiz": 8, "showExplanationsAfterSubmit": true}

title: Payroll Overview
summary: What payroll features are available in Forge: employee self-service, pay stubs, tax documents, and admin upload workflows.
contentJson:
{"body": "## Payroll Overview

Forge provides employee self-service payroll features so every team member can view their earnings, deductions, and year-end tax documents without bothering HR or management. Admins and Office Managers can upload documents on behalf of employees, and if a QuickBooks Payroll integration is connected, records sync automatically.

### What's Included

The payroll module lives under **Account** in the sidebar and has two pages:

- **Pay Stubs** — A chronological list of every pay period showing pay date, period range, gross pay, deductions, and net pay. Each stub can have a PDF attachment for download.
- **Tax Documents** — Year-end tax forms (W-2, W-2c, 1099-MISC, 1099-NEC, and Other) organized by tax year with PDF downloads.

### Employee Self-Service

Every employee can access their own records at any time:

1. Navigate to **Account → Pay Stubs** to see all pay stubs sorted by most recent pay date first.
2. Navigate to **Account → Tax Documents** to see all tax documents sorted by tax year.
3. Click the download icon on any row that has a PDF attachment to view or save the document.

Employees can only see their own records — they cannot view pay information for other users.

### Admin Upload

Admins and Office Managers can upload payroll documents for any employee:

- **Pay Stub Upload** — Provide pay period start/end dates, pay date, gross pay, net pay, and an optional PDF file attachment. The system calculates total deductions as the difference between gross and net pay.
- **Tax Document Upload** — Select the document type (W-2, W-2c, 1099-MISC, 1099-NEC, or Other), specify the tax year, and attach the PDF.

Manually uploaded documents show a gray 'Manual' chip in the Source column. Documents synced from an accounting provider show a blue 'Synced' chip.

### How Payroll Connects to Other Features

**Accounting Integration:** When QuickBooks Payroll (or another provider) is connected, pay stubs and tax documents sync automatically. Synced records are read-only and show the 'Synced' source badge.

**Employee Profile:** Pay stubs are tied to each user's account. Admins can view and manage payroll records from the employee administration panel.

**File Storage:** PDF attachments for pay stubs and tax documents are stored in MinIO (the same secure file storage used across the platform). Downloads are served directly from the API.

### Pay Stub Deductions

Each pay stub can include itemized deductions across 15 categories:

- **Taxes:** Federal Tax, State Tax, Social Security, Medicare
- **Insurance:** Health Insurance, Dental, Vision, Life Insurance, Disability Insurance
- **Savings:** 401(k) Retirement, HSA, FSA
- **Other:** Union Dues, Garnishment/Child Support, Other

Deductions are visible in the pay stub detail and sum to the Total Deductions amount shown in the table.

### When to Use Payroll Features

- When you need to check your latest pay stub or verify a deduction
- When downloading a W-2 or 1099 for tax filing season
- When an admin needs to manually upload a corrected pay stub (W-2c)
- When onboarding a new accounting integration and verifying synced records match", "sections": []}

ParkingBenefit is not one of them — it would fall under 'Other'."}, {"id": "pr5", "text": "An employee needs their W-2 for tax filing. Which fields are required?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Pay Date, Gross Pay, and a PDF attachment"}, {"id": "b", "text": "Pay Period Start, Pay Period End, Pay Date, Gross Pay, and Net Pay", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "Employee name, Pay Date, Net Pay, and deduction breakdown"}, {"id": "d", "text": "Pay Date, Gross Pay, Net Pay, Federal Tax, and State Tax amounts"}], "explanation": "The required fields for uploading a pay stub are: Pay Period Start, Pay Period End, Pay Date, Gross Pay, and Net Pay. The PDF attachment is optional, and Total Deductions is calculated automatically as the difference between Gross Pay and Net Pay."}, {"id": "pr8", "text": "Can an employee delete their own pay stubs or tax documents?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Yes, they can delete any of their own records"}, {"id": "b", "text": "Yes, but only manually uploaded ones"}, {"id": "c", "text": "No — only Admins and Office Managers can delete payroll records", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "d", "text": "Yes, but only after the tax year has closed"}], "explanation": "Employees have view-only access to their own payroll records. Only Admins and Office Managers can upload or delete payroll documents, and synced records from an accounting provider cannot be deleted at all."}, {"id": "pr9", "text": "What type of tax document would you expect to see for an independent contractor?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "W-2"}, {"id": "b", "text": "W-2c"}, {"id": "c", "text": "1099-NEC", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "d", "text": "1099-MISC"}], "explanation": "Independent contractors receive a 1099-NEC (Nonemployee Compensation) for payments of $600 or more. The 1099-MISC is used for other types of miscellaneous income like rents and royalties, not for contractor compensation."}, {"id": "pr10", "text": "A pay stub's download icon is missing from the Actions column. What does this mean?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "The pay stub has been archived and is no longer accessible"}, {"id": "b", "text": "The pay stub does not have a PDF file attachment", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "You don't have permission to download this particular stub"}, {"id": "d", "text": "The file server is offline and downloads are temporarily unavailable"}], "explanation": "The download icon only appears when the pay stub has an associated PDF file attachment (FileAttachmentId is not null). Some synced records may not include a PDF depending on the provider, and manual uploads may have been created without attaching a file."}], "passingScore": 80, "shuffleOptions": true, "questionsPerQuiz": 8, "showExplanationsAfterSubmit": true}

title: Planning Cycles Overview
summary: What planning cycles are, the two-week cadence, Day 1 planning flow, daily Top 3, and end-of-cycle rollover.
contentJson:
{"body": "## Planning Cycles Overview

Planning Cycles bring structure to your shop's workflow by organizing backlog jobs into focused, time-boxed sprints. Instead of an ever-growing list of work, you commit a manageable set of jobs to a cycle, track progress, and review results at the end.

### What Is a Planning Cycle?

A planning cycle is a fixed-duration window — typically two weeks (14 days) — during which your team commits to completing a specific set of jobs. Each cycle has:
- A **name** (e.g., 'Sprint 12' or 'Week of Mar 11')
- A **start date** and **end date**
- Optional **goals** describing what the team aims to accomplish
- A **status** that progresses through the lifecycle: Planning → Active → Completed

### The Two-Week Cadence

The default cycle length is 14 days, but you can set any duration when creating a cycle. The system calculates duration automatically from your start and end dates. Most shops find two-week cycles hit the sweet spot — long enough to make meaningful progress, short enough to course-correct quickly.

### Day 1: Planning Day

The first day of each cycle is Planning Day. This is when you:
1. **Review the backlog** — the left panel shows all uncommitted jobs from the backlog, filterable by search and priority
2. **Commit jobs** — drag jobs from the backlog panel on the left into the cycle panel on the right, or click the + button on each backlog job
3. **Set goals** — describe what the team aims to accomplish this cycle
4. **Prioritize** — reorder committed entries by dragging them up or down in the cycle panel
5. **Activate the cycle** — move it from Planning to Active status when the team is aligned

### The Split-Panel Layout

The planning page uses a split-panel design:
- **Left panel (Backlog):** Shows all jobs that haven't been committed to the current cycle. You can search by job number or title and filter by priority. Jobs already committed to the selected cycle are automatically hidden from the backlog.
- **Right panel (Cycle Board):** Shows the selected cycle's details — name, date range, goals, progress bar, and the list of committed entries with their completion status.

Drag a job from the left panel and drop it on the right panel to commit it. The backlog list updates instantly to hide the committed job.

### Daily Top 3

Each evening, the system prompts team members to identify their Top 3 priorities for the next day. This lightweight daily check-in keeps focus sharp without the overhead of a full standup meeting. The Top 3 feeds into the cycle board so everyone can see what's being worked on.

### Tracking Progress

Once a cycle is active, you track progress directly on the cycle board:
- **Mark entries complete** — click the check circle icon on any entry to mark it done
- **Progress bar** — shows the percentage of completed entries (e.g., '5 / 12' and '42%')
- **Days remaining** — displayed when the cycle is active, counting down to the end date
- **Rolled-over badge** — entries that came from a previous cycle show a replay icon so you can see carryover work

### End-of-Cycle: Completion and Rollover

When the cycle ends, you have two options:

1. **Complete & Roll Over** — marks the current cycle as Completed and automatically creates a new cycle. Any incomplete entries are carried over to the new cycle with the 'rolled over' badge. The new cycle starts the day after the old one ends and uses the same duration.

2. **Complete (No Rollover)** — marks the cycle as Completed. Incomplete entries return to the backlog. No new cycle is created automatically.

Both options require confirmation via a dialog to prevent accidental completion.

### Cycle Statuses

| Status | Meaning |
|--------|--------|
| **Planning** | Cycle is being assembled. Jobs can be added, removed, and reordered freely. Not yet started. |
| **Active** | Cycle is in progress. Jobs can still be added or removed, but the team is actively working. Shows days remaining. |
| **Completed** | Cycle is finished. Read-only. All entries show their final completion state. |

### When to Use Planning Cycles

- When your backlog has more work than your team can tackle at once
- When you want to create focus and commitment around a set of deliverables
- When you need visibility into what was planned vs. what was actually completed
- When you want to track how much work rolls over between cycles (a signal of over-commitment)", "sections": []}

title: Production Lots Overview
summary: What production lots are, why traceability matters, and how lots connect to parts, jobs, purchase orders, and QC inspections.
contentJson:
{"body": "## Production Lots Overview

A production lot is a tracked batch of parts that share the same manufacturing conditions, material source, or receiving event. Lot tracking gives you the ability to trace every unit back to its origin — which job produced it, which purchase order supplied the raw material, which production run it came from, and which QC inspections it passed.

### Why Lot Tracking Matters

Lot traceability is essential for:
- **Recalls** — If a defect is discovered in a batch of parts, you need to know exactly which customers received units from that lot, which jobs used them, and where remaining stock is stored.
- **FDA/Medical compliance** — Regulated industries (medical devices, aerospace, food-contact) require full lot traceability from raw material receipt through final shipment. Forge's lot system satisfies 21 CFR Part 820 and ISO 13485 traceability requirements.
- **Supplier accountability** — When incoming material is defective, the supplier lot number links the problem back to the vendor's batch, supporting return-to-vendor claims.
- **Quality investigations** — QC failures can be correlated to specific lots, revealing patterns like a bad material batch or a machine that drifted out of tolerance during a particular run.

### What a Lot Record Contains

Each lot record captures:
- **Lot Number** — A unique identifier, either manually assigned or auto-generated (format: `LOT-YYYYMMDD-NNN`, e.g., `LOT-20260415-001`).
- **Part** — The part this lot is associated with. Every lot must reference exactly one part.
- **Quantity** — How many units are in this lot.
- **Job** (optional) — The production job that created these parts.
- **Production Run** (optional) — The specific production run within a job.
- **Purchase Order Line** (optional) — If the lot was received from a vendor, the PO line that sourced it.
- **Supplier Lot Number** (optional) — The vendor's own lot/batch number for incoming material.
- **Expiration Date** (optional) — For materials with a shelf life (adhesives, chemicals, perishable components).
- **Notes** (optional) — Free-form notes about the lot (storage conditions, deviations, special handling).

### How Lots Connect to Other Features

**Parts:** Every lot is tied to a specific part number. You can query all lots for a given part to see its full production and receiving history.

**Jobs:** When a job produces parts, a lot record links the output back to the job. This answers the question: 'Which job made these specific units?'

**Purchase Orders:** When material arrives from a vendor, a lot record ties the received quantity to the PO line. The supplier lot number preserves the vendor's own batch identifier.

**Production Runs:** Lots can be linked to specific production runs, providing finer granularity than job-level tracking.

**QC Inspections:** Quality inspections reference lot numbers. The traceability query shows all inspections performed on a given lot.

**Inventory (Bin Locations):** Bin contents can be tracked by lot number, so you know exactly which lot is stored in which bin.

### When to Create Lots

- When receiving material from a vendor (link to PO line + supplier lot number)
- When a production run completes a batch of parts (link to job + production run)
- When splitting or combining batches for inventory management
- When regulated processes require batch-level documentation", "sections": []}

title: Production Lots — Tracking Workflow
summary: A conceptual walkthrough of the lot tracking workflow: creating lots, querying traceability, and handling recalls.
contentJson:
{"body": "## Production Lots — Tracking Workflow

Production lots are managed via the API and integrated into the Quality module. This walkthrough covers the three core workflows: creating lots, querying traceability, and handling a recall scenario.

### Workflow 1: Creating a Lot on Material Receipt

When raw material arrives from a vendor:

1. **Receive the shipment** against the purchase order in the Inventory module.
2. **A lot record is created** linking the received quantity to the PO line. The lot number is auto-generated (e.g., `LOT-20260415-001`) or you can assign a custom lot number.
3. **Record the supplier lot number** — this is the vendor's own batch identifier printed on their packing slip or certificate of analysis.
4. **Set an expiration date** if the material has a shelf life (adhesives, chemicals, rubber compounds).
5. The lot is now traceable: you can look up this lot number and see which vendor supplied it, on which PO, and when it was received.

### Workflow 2: Creating a Lot During Production

When your shop produces parts:

1. **A production run completes** a batch of parts on a job.
2. **A lot record is created** linking the output to the job and production run. The quantity reflects how many good parts came off the run.
3. **QC inspection is performed** on a sample from the lot. The inspection references the lot number.
4. **Parts are stored** in bin locations tagged with the lot number, so inventory knows exactly which lot is in which bin.

### Workflow 3: Traceability Query

The traceability query is the core of the lot system. Given a lot number, it returns the complete history:

- **Part information** — which part number and description this lot represents
- **Jobs** — all jobs that produced or consumed parts from this lot
- **Production runs** — specific production runs linked to the lot
- **Purchase orders** — the PO and vendor that supplied the raw material (with vendor name)
- **Bin locations** — current inventory locations and quantities for this lot
- **QC inspections** — all quality inspections performed on this lot, with inspector name, status, and date

This single query gives you the full chain from raw material receipt through production, inspection, storage, and shipment.

### Workflow 4: Handling a Recall

When a defect is discovered in shipped parts:

1. **Identify the lot number** from the defective part's label, shipping record, or customer complaint.
2. **Run a traceability query** on that lot number to see the full history.
3. **Check bin locations** — are there remaining units from this lot still in inventory? If so, quarantine them.
4. **Review QC inspections** — did the inspection catch any anomalies? Were acceptance criteria met?
5. **Trace the supplier** — if the root cause is raw material, the supplier lot number identifies the vendor's batch. Contact the vendor with this information.
6. **Identify affected customers** — cross-reference the lot's jobs with shipment records to find which customers received parts from this lot.
7. **Document the investigation** — add notes to the lot record and related QC inspections.

### Lot Number Format

Auto-generated lot numbers follow the pattern `LOT-YYYYMMDD-NNN`:
- `LOT` — fixed prefix
- `YYYYMMDD` — date the lot was created
- `NNN` — sequential counter for that day (001, 002, 003...)

Example: `LOT-20260415-003` is the third lot created on April 15, 2026.

Custom lot numbers are also supported — simply provide your own lot number when creating the record. This is useful when adopting a customer's or industry's lot numbering scheme.

### Best Practices

- **Always record supplier lot numbers** when receiving material. This is your link back to the vendor if a material issue surfaces months later.
- **Create lots at the finest practical granularity.** One lot per production run is ideal. One lot per entire job is acceptable for low-risk parts.
- **Set expiration dates** on any material with a shelf life. Expired lots should not be used in production.
- **Link lots to QC inspections.** Even if your process doesn't require formal lot tracking, linking inspections to lots makes investigations dramatically faster.
- **Use the traceability query regularly** — don't wait for a recall. Periodic traceability audits catch gaps before they matter.", "sections": []}

title: Production Lots Field Reference
summary: Complete reference for lot record fields, API endpoints, traceability query parameters, and validation rules.
contentJson: {"title": "Production Lots Field Reference", "groups": [{"items": [{"label": "Lot Number", "value": "String, max 100 characters. Auto-generated if not provided (format: LOT-YYYYMMDD-NNN, e.g., LOT-20260415-001). Can be manually assigned for custom numbering schemes."}, {"label": "Part ID (required)", "value": "Integer, must be greater than 0. References the part this lot is associated with. Every lot must be tied to exactly one part."}, {"label": "Quantity (required)", "value": "Integer, must be greater than 0. The number of units in this lot."}, {"label": "Job ID (optional)", "value": "Integer. References the production job that created this lot. Null for lots created from vendor receipts without a job."}, {"label": "Production Run ID (optional)", "value": "Integer. References the specific production run within a job. Provides finer granularity than job-level tracking."}, {"label": "Purchase Order Line ID (optional)", "value": "Integer. References the PO line that sourced this lot. Used when lot is created from receiving vendor material."}, {"label": "Supplier Lot Number (optional)", "value": "String, max 100 characters. The vendor's own batch/lot identifier. Critical for supplier accountability and recall investigations."}, {"label": "Expiration Date (optional)", "value": "DateTimeOffset (UTC). For materials with shelf life. Format: ISO 8601. Null for non-perishable materials."}, {"label": "Notes (optional)", "value": "String, max 2000 characters. Free-form notes: storage conditions, deviations, special handling instructions."}, {"label": "Created At", "value": "DateTimeOffset (UTC). Auto-set when the lot record is created. Used for chronological sorting."}, {"label": "Created By", "value": "String (User ID). Auto-set from the authenticated user. Query parameters: partId (int, optional), jobId (int, optional), search (string, optional). Search matches against lot number, supplier lot number, and part number. Returns list sorted by CreatedAt descending."}, {"label": "POST /api/v1/lots", "value": "Create a new lot record. Returns 201 with Location header. Auto-generates lot number if not provided."}, {"label": "GET /api/v1/lots/{lotNumber}/trace", "value": "Traceability query. Purchase orders that sourced this lot's material."}, {"label": "binLocations[]", "value": "Array of { locationId, locationName, quantity }. Current inventory locations holding this lot."}, {"label": "inspections[]", "value": "Array of { id, status, inspectorName, createdAt }. QC inspections performed on this lot."}], "heading": "Traceability Query Response"}, {"items": [{"label": "Part ID", "value": "Required. Must be greater than 0. Must reference an existing part."}, {"label": "Quantity", "value": "Required. Must be greater than 0."}, {"label": "Lot Number", "value": "Optional. Max 100 characters. If omitted, auto-generated as LOT-YYYYMMDD-NNN."}, {"label": "Supplier Lot Number", "value": "Optional. Max 100 characters."}, {"label": "Notes", "value": "Optional. Max 2000 characters."}, {"label": "Expiration Date", "value": "Optional. Must be a valid ISO 8601 date if provided."}], "heading": "Validation Rules"}, {"items": [{"label": "Required Roles", "value": "Admin, Manager, Engineer, or Production Worker. All three endpoints share the same role requirement."}, {"label": "Soft Deletes", "value": "Lot records use soft delete (DeletedAt timestamp). Deleted lots are excluded from queries by the global query filter."}], "heading": "Authorization"}, {"items": [{"label": "Pattern", "value": "LOT-YYYYMMDD-NNN"}, {"label": "Prefix", "value": "LOT- (fixed)"}, {"label": "Date segment", "value": "YYYYMMDD — the UTC date when the lot is created (e.g., 20260415)"}, {"label": "Sequence", "value": "NNN — zero-padded 3-digit counter, incremented per day (001, 002, ... 999)"}, {"label": "Example", "value": "LOT-20260415-003 = third lot created on April 15, 2026"}, {"label": "Custom override", "value": "Provide your own lotNumber in the POST body to skip auto-generation. Useful for customer-mandated or industry-specific numbering."}], "heading": "Auto-Generated Lot Number Format"}, {"items": [{"label": "Part", "value": "Required FK. The part this lot tracks. Navigation: Part.PartNumber, Part.Description."}, {"label": "Job", "value": "Optional FK. The production job that created or consumed this lot. Navigation: Job.JobNumber, Job.Title."}, {"label": "ProductionRun", "value": "Optional FK. The specific production run. Navigation: ProductionRun.RunNumber, ProductionRun.Status."}, {"label": "PurchaseOrderLine", "value": "Optional FK. The PO line for received material. Navigation: PurchaseOrderLine.PurchaseOrder.PONumber, PurchaseOrder.Vendor.CompanyName."}, {"label": "BinContent", "value": "Inventory bin contents can reference lot numbers. Matched by EntityType='part', EntityId=PartId, LotNumber."}, {"label": "QcInspection", "value": "Quality inspections reference lot numbers via the LotNumber field. Matched directly by lot number string."}], "heading": "Related Entities"}]}

title: Production Lots Knowledge Check
summary: Test your knowledge of production lots: traceability, lot numbers, API usage, FDA/medical compliance, and recall workflows.
contentJson: {"questions": [{"id": "pl1", "text": "What is the primary purpose of production lot tracking in Forge?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "To count how many parts are in inventory at any given time"}, {"id": "b", "text": "To trace batches of parts back to their origin for recall capability, quality investigations, and regulatory compliance", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "To schedule production runs on the shop floor"}, {"id": "d", "text": "To calculate the cost of goods sold for each part"}], "explanation": "Lot tracking provides full traceability from raw material receipt through production, inspection, and shipment. This is essential for recalls, FDA/medical compliance, supplier accountability, and quality investigations."}, {"id": "pl2", "text": "A lot record must always be linked to which of the following?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "A job and a production run"}, {"id": "b", "text": "A purchase order line and a supplier lot number"}, {"id": "c", "text": "A part — every lot must reference exactly one part", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "d", "text": "A QC inspection and a bin location"}], "explanation": "Part ID is the only required relationship. Every lot must be tied to exactly one part. Job, production run, purchase order line, and supplier lot number are all optional and depend on how the lot was created."}, {"id": "pl3", "text": "What format does Forge use for auto-generated lot numbers?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "BATCH-{partNumber}-{sequence}"}, {"id": "b", "text": "LOT-YYYYMMDD-NNN (e.g., LOT-20260415-001)", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "LR-{year}-{globalSequence}"}, {"id": "d", "text": "{partNumber}-{date}-{random}"}], "explanation": "Auto-generated lot numbers follow LOT-YYYYMMDD-NNN: a fixed 'LOT-' prefix, the UTC creation date, and a zero-padded daily sequence number. Custom lot numbers can be provided to override this format."}, {"id": "pl4", "text": "You receive raw material from a vendor. Which fields should you populate on the lot record?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Part ID, quantity, purchase order line ID, and supplier lot number", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "b", "text": "Part ID, quantity, job ID, and production run ID"}, {"id": "c", "text": "Part ID, quantity, and expiration date only"}, {"id": "d", "text": "Only lot number and quantity — the system fills in the rest"}], "explanation": "For vendor-received material, link the lot to the PO line (purchase order line ID) and record the supplier's own batch identifier (supplier lot number). Job and production run are for internally produced lots."}, {"id": "pl5", "text": "A customer reports a defect in parts they received. What is the first step in the recall workflow?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Shut down all production lines immediately"}, {"id": "b", "text": "Identify the lot number from the part's label, shipping record, or customer complaint, then run a traceability query", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "Contact the vendor and request a replacement shipment"}, {"id": "d", "text": "Delete the lot record to prevent further shipments"}], "explanation": "The first step is identifying the lot number and running a traceability query. This reveals the full chain: which jobs produced it, where remaining stock is stored, which QC inspections were performed, and which supplier material was used."}, {"id": "pl6", "text": "What information does the traceability query (GET /api/v1/lots/{lotNumber}/trace) return?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Only the lot record fields and the associated part number"}, {"id": "b", "text": "The part info, linked jobs, production runs, purchase orders with vendor names, bin locations with quantities, and QC inspections with inspector details", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "A list of all customers who received parts from this lot"}, {"id": "d", "text": "The financial cost breakdown of producing this lot"}], "explanation": "The traceability query returns the complete chain: part information, all linked jobs, production runs, purchase orders (with vendor name), current bin locations (with quantities), and QC inspections (with inspector name, status, and date)."}, {"id": "pl7", "text": "When should you set an expiration date on a lot record?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Always — every lot must have an expiration date for compliance"}, {"id": "b", "text": "When the material has a shelf life, such as adhesives, chemicals, or rubber compounds", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "Only for lots received from vendors, never for internally produced lots"}, {"id": "d", "text": "Only when the customer specifically requests lot expiration tracking"}], "explanation": "Expiration dates are for materials with a shelf life — adhesives, chemicals, perishable components. The field is optional and should be null for non-perishable materials like metal stock or hardware."}, {"id": "pl8", "text": "Which roles have access to the lot management API endpoints?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Admin and Manager only"}, {"id": "b", "text": "Admin, Manager, Engineer, and Production Worker", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "Admin, Manager, and Engineer — Production Workers have read-only access"}, {"id": "d", "text": "All roles including Office Manager and PM"}], "explanation": "The LotsController is authorized for Admin, Manager, Engineer, and Production Worker roles. All four roles can create lots and run traceability queries."}, {"id": "pl9", "text": "How are lot records deleted in Forge?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Hard deleted — the database row is permanently removed"}, {"id": "b", "text": "Soft deleted using a DeletedAt timestamp — the record is preserved but excluded from queries", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "Archived to a separate history table"}, {"id": "d", "text": "Lot records cannot be deleted under any circumstances"}], "explanation": "Like all entities extending BaseEntity, lot records use soft delete via the DeletedAt timestamp. The global query filter automatically excludes deleted lots from all queries, but the data is preserved for audit trail and compliance."}, {"id": "pl10", "text": "You want to find all lots for a specific part that were received from vendors. How do you query the API?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "GET /api/v1/lots?partId={id} — then filter results client-side for those with a purchaseOrderLineId", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "b", "text": "GET /api/v1/lots/{partId}/vendor-lots"}, {"id": "c", "text": "GET /api/v1/parts/{id}/lots?source=vendor"}, {"id": "d", "text": "GET /api/v1/lots/trace?partId={id}&type=received"}], "explanation": "The GET /api/v1/lots endpoint accepts a partId query parameter to filter by part. The response includes purchaseOrderLineId and supplierLotNumber fields — lots with non-null values for these were received from vendors. There is no dedicated vendor-lot endpoint."}], "passingScore": 80, "shuffleOptions": true, "questionsPerQuiz": 8, "showExplanationsAfterSubmit": true}

title: Purchase Orders Overview
summary: What purchase orders are, the PO lifecycle from draft to close, line items, receiving workflow, blanket POs, and how POs connect to vendors, inventory, and jobs.
contentJson:
{"body": "## Purchase Orders Overview

The Purchase Orders module manages your procurement workflow — from requesting materials to receiving them on your dock. Every purchase order (PO) tracks what you ordered, from which vendor, how much you paid, and what has arrived so far.

### What Is a Purchase Order?

A purchase order is a formal document sent to a vendor authorizing them to supply specific parts at agreed prices. Each PO contains:
- **Header** — vendor, optional linked job, expected delivery date, notes
- **Line items** — one or more parts with ordered quantity and unit price
- **Receiving records** — what has actually arrived against each line

### PO Lifecycle

Every purchase order moves through a defined status workflow:

1. **Draft** — PO is being prepared. Lines can be added, edited, or removed. The PO has not been sent to the vendor yet.
2. **Submitted** — PO has been sent to the vendor. You are waiting for their acknowledgment.
3. **Acknowledged** — The vendor has confirmed the order. You can now begin receiving items.
4. **Partially Received** — Some line items have been received, but not all. The system tracks received vs. remaining quantities per line.
5. **Received** — All line items are fully received. The PO is complete.
6. **Closed** — The PO has been formally closed after receiving is complete. No further receiving is possible.
7. **Cancelled** — The PO was cancelled before completion. Only Draft, Submitted, and Acknowledged POs can be cancelled.

### Line Items

Each line item on a PO represents a specific part you are ordering:
- **Part** — selected from your Parts Catalog (assemblies are excluded)
- **Ordered Quantity** — how many units you need
- **Unit Price** — cost per unit (auto-fills from the part's default price if available)
- **Line Total** — automatically calculated as quantity times unit price
- **Received Quantity** — updated as items arrive
- **Remaining Quantity** — ordered minus received, calculated automatically

The PO total is the sum of all line totals.

### Receiving Workflow

When materials arrive, you open the Receive Items dialog from the PO detail:
1. The dialog shows all lines that still have remaining quantity
2. Enter the quantity received for each line (or click Receive All to fill in remaining quantities)
3. Save to record the receipt

Receiving updates the line's received quantity and may change the PO status:
- If some lines still have remaining quantity → **Partially Received**
- If all lines are fully received → **Received**

Each receiving event creates a receiving record with the quantity, who received it, and optional notes.

### Blanket Purchase Orders

A blanket PO is a long-term agreement with a vendor for a total quantity at an agreed unit price. Instead of ordering everything at once, you create releases against the blanket PO over time:
- **Blanket Total Quantity** — the total committed quantity
- **Agreed Unit Price** — the negotiated price per unit
- **Blanket Expiration Date** — when the agreement expires
- **Releases** — individual delivery requests drawn against the blanket quantity

Each release specifies a line item, quantity, and requested delivery date. Releases have their own status tracking (Open, Sent, Partial Received, Received, Cancelled).

### How POs Connect to Other Features

**Vendors:** Every PO is linked to a vendor. The vendor dropdown on the PO form pulls from your Vendors module. Clicking the vendor name in the PO detail opens the vendor record.

**Jobs:** A PO can optionally be linked to a specific job (e.g., ordering materials for a production run). The job link appears in the PO detail and list table.

**Parts Catalog:** PO line items reference parts from your Parts Catalog. When selecting a part, the unit price auto-fills from the part's default price if one exists.

**Inventory:** Receiving items against a PO can update inventory quantities when a storage location is specified on the receiving record.

**Kanban Board:** When a job on the kanban board reaches the 'Materials Ordered' stage, the associated PO tracks what was ordered and when it arrives.

### When to Use Purchase Orders

- When ordering raw materials, components, or supplies from a vendor
- When you need to track what has been ordered vs. what has arrived
- When linking material purchases to specific jobs for cost tracking
- When setting up blanket agreements for recurring material needs
- When you need a paper trail for procurement (PDF export available)", "sections": []}

Use the gear icon to customize visible columns."}}, {"element": "app-data-table thead th:nth-child(5)", "popover": {"side": "bottom", "title": "Status Column", "description": "Status is shown as a color-coded chip: gray for Draft, blue for Submitted, purple for Acknowledged, orange for Partially Received, green for Received, gray for Closed, and red for Cancelled. summary: Test your knowledge of the Purchase Orders module: lifecycle, receiving, line items, blanket POs, and common workflows.
contentJson: {"questions": [{"id": "po1", "text": "You need to order 500 aluminum brackets from a vendor for an upcoming production run. Receiving is not available until the vendor acknowledges the order. Delete is only available for Draft POs."}, {"id": "po3", "text": "You submitted a PO to your vendor and they confirmed receipt. What status should you set the PO to?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Partially Received — since the vendor received it"}, {"id": "b", "text": "Acknowledged — the vendor has confirmed the order", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "Received — because the vendor received the PO document"}, {"id": "d", "text": "Closed — the submission process is complete"}], "explanation": "Acknowledged means the vendor has confirmed the order. This is different from Received, which means your shop has received the physical materials. The Acknowledge action is available when the PO is in Submitted status."}, {"id": "po4", "text": "A PO has 3 line items. You receive items for 2 of them, but the third hasn't arrived yet. What status does the PO move to?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Received — because you recorded a receiving event"}, {"id": "b", "text": "Partially Received — some lines are fulfilled but not all", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "Acknowledged — it stays here until everything arrives"}, {"id": "d", "text": "Closed — the received items are automatically closed"}], "explanation": "When some line items have been received but others still have remaining quantity, the PO moves to Partially Received. It stays in this status until all lines are fully received."}, {"id": "po5", "text": "When adding a line item to a PO, you select a part and the unit price auto-fills to $12.50. Where does this price come from?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "The vendor's quoted price from the last PO"}, {"id": "b", "text": "The part's default price in the Parts Catalog", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "The average of all previous purchase prices for this part"}, {"id": "d", "text": "A fixed system-wide default price for all parts"}], "explanation": "When you select a part on a PO line, the unit price auto-fills from the part's default price if one is set in the Parts Catalog. A tooltip indicates the price is from the part's list price. You can manually override this price."}, {"id": "po6", "text": "You want to cancel a PO that is in Partially Received status. Is this possible?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Yes — any PO can be cancelled at any time"}, {"id": "b", "text": "No — only Draft, Submitted, and Acknowledged POs can be cancelled", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "Yes — but only if you return the received items first"}, {"id": "d", "text": "No — you must close it instead of cancelling"}], "explanation": "The Cancel action is only available for POs in Draft, Submitted, or Acknowledged status. Once items have been received (Partially Received or Received), the PO cannot be cancelled — it must proceed through the receiving workflow to Closed."}, {"id": "po7", "text": "In the Receive Items dialog, what does the 'Receive All' button do?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Marks the entire PO as Received regardless of actual quantities"}, {"id": "b", "text": "Fills in the remaining quantity for every receivable line item", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "Creates a new receiving record with zero quantities for audit purposes"}, {"id": "d", "text": "Closes the PO and archives all line items"}], "explanation": "Receive All is a convenience button that fills in the remaining quantity for every line that still has outstanding items. This is useful when a complete shipment arrives and you don't need to enter quantities line by line. You still need to click Save to record the receipt."}, {"id": "po8", "text": "What is a blanket purchase order?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "A PO that covers all parts from a single vendor at once"}, {"id": "b", "text": "A long-term agreement for a total quantity at an agreed price, with individual releases over time", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "A PO template that auto-generates new POs on a schedule"}, {"id": "d", "text": "A PO that has been approved by management for large expenditures"}], "explanation": "A blanket PO is a long-term purchasing agreement with a vendor. You commit to a total quantity at an agreed unit price, then create individual releases over time as you need materials delivered. Each release specifies a quantity and requested delivery date."}, {"id": "po9", "text": "A PO line item shows Ordered: 100, Received: 75, Remaining: 25. What does this tell you?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "The vendor has shipped all 100 units but 25 were rejected during inspection"}, {"id": "b", "text": "75 units have arrived and been recorded; 25 units are still outstanding", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "You originally ordered 75 but increased the order to 100"}, {"id": "d", "text": "25 units are on backorder and the rest were cancelled"}], "explanation": "Remaining Quantity is calculated as Ordered minus Received. Here, 75 units have been physically received and recorded through the Receive Items workflow. 25 units are still expected from the vendor."}, {"id": "po10", "text": "You need to delete a purchase order. Under what condition is this allowed?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Any PO can be deleted at any time by an admin"}, {"id": "b", "text": "Only POs in Draft status can be deleted", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "POs can be deleted only after they are Cancelled"}, {"id": "d", "text": "POs are never deleted — they can only be archived"}], "explanation": "The Delete action is only available for Draft POs. Once a PO has been submitted or progressed further in the lifecycle, it cannot be deleted — it can only be cancelled (if eligible) or closed. Delete requires a confirmation dialog with danger severity."}, {"id": "po11", "text": "You want to see all POs from a specific vendor that are waiting for materials. Which filters do you apply?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Set the Vendor filter to the vendor name and Status filter to Acknowledged or Partially Received", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "b", "text": "Search for the vendor name in the search box — no other filters needed"}, {"id": "c", "text": "Set the Status filter to Submitted and sort by vendor name"}, {"id": "d", "text": "Go to the vendor's detail page and look at the PO tab"}], "explanation": "Use the Vendor dropdown to select the specific vendor, then set the Status filter. POs that are 'waiting for materials' are in Acknowledged status (vendor confirmed, awaiting shipment) or Partially Received (some items arrived, still waiting for more)."}, {"id": "po12", "text": "After all items on a PO are received, what is the next step to finalize the order?", "options": [{"id": "a", "text": "Delete the PO to clear it from the list"}, {"id": "b", "text": "Click Close PO to formally close the order", "isCorrect": true}, {"id": "c", "text": "The PO automatically closes — no action needed"}, {"id": "d", "text": "Submit the PO again to confirm completion with the vendor"}], "explanation": "When all items are received, the PO moves to Received status. The final step is to click Close PO, which formally closes the order. This is a deliberate action — POs do not auto-close. Once closed, no further actions (receiving, cancelling) are possible."}], "passingScore": 80, "shuffleOptions": true, "questionsPerQuiz": 8, "showExplanationsAfterSubmit": true}

title: Quality Overview
summary: What the Quality module does: QC templates, inspections, lot tracking, SPC, NCRs, CAPAs, ECOs, and gages — and how quality connects to jobs, parts, and production.
contentJson:
{"body": "## Quality Overview

The Quality module is your central hub for quality control and assurance across the entire manufacturing process. It brings together inspections, lot traceability, statistical process control (SPC), non-conformance reports (NCRs), corrective/preventive actions (CAPAs), engineering change orders (ECOs), and gage management — all in one place.

### QC Templates

Templates define reusable checklists of inspection checks. Each template has a name, optional description, and an optional link to a specific part. Templates contain checklist items — each item has a description, an optional specification (the expected value or tolerance), a sort order, and a required flag. When you create an inspection, you can select a template and the checklist items pre-populate the inspection.

### QC Inspections

Inspections are the core quality records. Each inspection is linked to an inspector (the person performing the check) and can optionally reference a job, a production run, a QC template, and a lot number. Inspections track their status — In Progress, Passed, or Failed — and contain individual inspection results. Each result records the check description, whether it passed, the measured value, and notes. The results summary column shows how many checks passed out of the total (e.g., 5/6).

### Lot Tracking

The Lots tab tracks material lot records for traceability. Each lot has a unique lot number, is linked to a part, and records quantity, optional job reference, supplier lot number, expiration date, and notes. The traceability feature lets you click any lot and see every linked record: jobs that used this lot, production runs, purchase orders, bin locations where the material is stored, and QC inspections performed against it. This is critical for recall management and regulatory compliance.

### SPC (Statistical Process Control)

SPC provides real-time process monitoring through three sub-tabs:
- **SPC Charts** — X-bar and Range control charts for any measured characteristic. Shows control limits (UCL, LCL, center line), spec limits (USL, LSL), and flags out-of-control points.
- **SPC Data Entry** — Record measurement subgroups for a selected characteristic. Each subgroup contains multiple readings; the system calculates mean, range, standard deviation, and detects OOC conditions automatically.
- **OOC Events** — Lists all out-of-control events with severity (Warning, Out of Control, Out of Spec), acknowledgment tracking, and the ability to create a CAPA directly from an OOC event.

SPC characteristics are defined per part and optionally per operation. Each characteristic specifies nominal value, upper/lower spec limits, sample size, measurement type (variable or attribute), unit of measure, decimal places, and gage reference. Process capability indices (Cp, Cpk, Pp, Ppk) are calculated automatically.

### NCRs (Non-Conformance Reports)

NCRs document quality failures. Each NCR has a unique NCR number, type (Internal, Supplier, or Customer), and is linked to a part. NCRs track the detection stage (Receiving, In Process, Final Inspection, Shipping, Customer, Audit), affected and defective quantities, containment actions, and disposition (Use As Is, Rework, Scrap, Return to Vendor, Sort & Screen, Concession). NCRs flow through statuses: Open, Under Review, Contained, Dispositioned, Closed. Material and labor cost impact are tracked for Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) analysis. NCRs can link to jobs, production runs, lots, sales orders, purchase orders, QC inspections, customers, and vendors.

### CAPAs (Corrective and Preventive Actions)

CAPAs drive systematic problem resolution. Each CAPA has a number, type (Corrective or Preventive), source type (NCR, Audit, Customer Complaint, OOC Event, Internal, Supplier), and goes through phases: Identification, Containment, Root Cause Analysis, Corrective Action, Verification, Effectiveness Check. Root cause methods include 5 Whys, Fishbone Diagram, Fault Tree, and 8D. CAPAs track tasks, owner assignment, priority, due dates, and effectiveness verification with a scheduled check date.

### ECOs (Engineering Change Orders)

ECOs manage controlled changes to parts, processes, and documentation. Each ECO has a number, title, description, change type (New, Revision, Obsolescence, Cost Reduction, Quality Improvement), priority (Low, Normal, High, Critical), and flows through statuses: Draft, Review, Approved, In Implementation, Implemented, Cancelled. ECOs track affected items (parts, BOMs, operations) with old/new values and implementation status.

### Gages

The Gages tab manages measurement instruments and their calibration lifecycle. Each gage has a number, description, type, manufacturer, model, serial number, and accuracy/range/resolution specifications. Gages track calibration interval (in days), last calibration date, and next calibration due date. Statuses include In Service, Due for Calibration, Out for Calibration, Out of Service, and Retired. Each gage maintains a history of calibration records with result (Pass, Fail, Adjusted, Out of Tolerance), lab name, certificate file, standards used, and as-found/as-left conditions.

### How Quality Connects to Other Features

**Jobs:** Inspections link to jobs so you can see all quality checks performed for a specific job. NCRs reference the job where the defect was found.

**Parts:** Templates and SPC characteristics are defined per part. NCRs identify the defective part. Lot records trace material back to specific parts.

**Lots:** Lot numbers thread through inspections, production runs, purchase orders, and bin locations — providing full material traceability from receipt to shipment.

**Scanner Integration:** The quality page responds to barcode scans. On the Inspections tab, a scan fills the search field. On the Lots tab, a scan fills the lot search field. This enables quick lookup of parts or lots on the shop floor.

### When to Use Quality

- When performing incoming, in-process, or final inspections
- When tracking material lots for traceability and recall readiness
- When monitoring process stability with SPC charts
- When documenting non-conformances and driving corrective actions
- When managing engineering changes to parts or processes
- When tracking gage calibration schedules and records", "sections": []}
